issue_title: Volume setter fails when passed a value not ending with .0 or .5
issue_body:
For some receivers if not all, volumes steps are 0.5dB: you can set -45dB, -45.5dB, but not -45.4dB. However, you *can* call the volume setter with arbitrary values like -45.4. It ends up in a failure and volume not being set:
```
17-01-07 18:19:42 ERROR (Thread-8) [rxv] Request <YAMAHA_AV cmd="PUT"><Main_Zone><Volume><Lvl><Val>-449</Val><Exp>1</Exp><Unit>dB</Unit></Lvl></Volume></Main_Zone></YAMAHA_AV> failed with b'<YAMAHA_AV rsp="PUT" RC="3"><Main_Zone><Volume><Lvl></Lvl></Volume></Main_Zone></YAMAHA_AV>'
```

I guess the [volume setter](https://github.com/wuub/rxv/blob/master/rxv/rxv.py#L422) should ensure the computed value is a multiple of 5, or take the closest multiple of five value.

I encountered this issue while using the yamaha component from home-assistant: home-assistant/home-assistant#5209

I'm not sure on the side on which this issue should be fixed, but I guess checking the input in rxv makes more sense. I can probably write a patch if that sounds like a good way to fix this issue.

issue_title: In 'live' testing scenarios argument passing to build_tests is convoluted and SSL may not work
issue_body:
If you want to use `build_tests` to create real TestCases against a live server it's likely you know the URL and that would be most convenient thing to pass instead of having to parse out the host, port and prefix (script_name) and then pass those.

In addition, if you have a URL you know if your server is SSL but the tests may not have been written to do SSL (with an `ssl: true` entry). Because of the test building process this is a bit awkward at the moment. It would be better to be able to say "yeah, this is SSL"  for the whole run.

issue_title: Erroneous input parsing with paired-reads + singletons
issue_body:
While running:
```
minimap2 -ax sr db.mmi read1.fq.gz read2.fq.gz singletons.fq.gz
```
and monitoring I/O using [pipe viewer (pv -d pid)](https://github.com/icetee/pv) I noticed a somewhat strange behavior.

`db.mmi` is read in chunks and for every chunk `read1.fq.gz`, `read2.fq.gz` and `single.fq.gz` are re-read. This is a bit wasteful since it has to decompress the inputs several times but not necessarily a problem.

However, `single.fq.gz` is much smaller than the other 2 files. `minimap2` seems to stop reading all inputs as soon as it reaches the end of **one** of them. Because of this `single.fq.gz` is read in its entirety while only the first 5-10% of`read1.fq.gz` and `read2.fq.gz` seem to be read at which point `minimap2` moves on to the next index chunk.

The documentation doesn't explicitly mention support for `single.fq` but `minimap2` didn't complain either and a result is still produced.

issue_title: Building libraries with multiple source files
issue_body:
Hey Stefano,

I think there is a bug when building a library that consists of more than one source file.  If the library is only one file, then it works, but if there are multiple files then it doesn't.  It seems like the object code for anything other than the main file is not getting archived in the library.  

Example (for my [fortran-astrodynamics-toolkit](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/Fortran-Astrodynamics-Toolkit) project): 
```Fortran
FoBiS.py build -compiler gnu -colors -cflags "-c -O2" -dbld ./lib/ -s ./src/ -dmod ./ -dobj ./ -t fortran_astrodynamics_toolkit.f90 -o libfat.a -mklib static
```
All the modules are compiled, but the resulting ```.a``` file doesn't contain any of the code from any module other than the main one.  What should happen is that all the .o files that were compiled should be added to the library (using something like ```ar -rcs libfat.a ./lib/*.o```).

issue_title: API
issue_body:
May be make it simpler

```python
model = nv.load_model(filename)
view.add_model(model)
view.add_trajectory(traj)
```

where
`traj` is derived class of `nglview.Trajectory` (so we can use pytraj/mdtraj/mdanalysis, ... together)
`load_model` will try to load pdb, mol2, ccp4, dx, ...

NGL uses `component` but `model` is shorter to write. :D

issue_title: Request: docs for developers
issue_body:
While the official documentation works good for explaining the basic usage of pyeda, i'm having problems to understand the core-concepts/core-design, needed to expand it.

**Simple example:**
I wanted to add my own cardinality-expression (use-case: replace exponentially-growing OneHot0-expression for many variables). A prototype-like approach, mimicking _OneHot0()_ looks like that (maybe buggy; theory and code based on [Sinz: Towards an Optimal CNF Encoding of Boolean Cardinality Constraints
](http://www.carstensinz.de/papers/CP-2005.pdf); maybe a more elegant description is possible using pyeda's bitvector-capabilities):

``` python
def AtMostN(*xs, k=1, auxvarname='ohc'):
    """
    Return an expression that means
    "at most k input functions are true".
    Returns cnf
    """
    xs = [Expression.box(x).node for x in xs]
    n = len(xs)
    terms = list()
    aux_vars = exprvars(auxvarname, (n-1)*k)

    def s(i,j,k):
        return aux_vars[i*k+j]

    # case: i=1
    terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(xs[0]), s(0,0,k)))
    for i in range(1,k):
        terms.append(exprnode.not_(s(0,i,k)))

    # general case
    for i in range(1,n-1):
        terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(xs[i]),s(i,0,k)))
        terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(s(i-1,0,k), s(i,0,k))))
        for u in range(1,k):
            terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(xs[i]), exprnode.not_(s(i-1,u-1,k)), s(i,u,k)))
            terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(s(i-1,u,k)), s(i,u,k)))
        terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(xs[i]), exprnode.not_(s(i-1,k-1,k))))

    terms.append(exprnode.or_(exprnode.not_(xs[i], exprnode.not_(s(n-2,k-1,k)))))

    y = exprnode.and_(*terms)
    return _expr(y)
```

This won't work because the auxiliary-vars introduced are not of type ExprNode. Sadly, the documentation doesn't give away anything about this internal stuff (no hits during search for "exprnode").

The next best thing would be to learn from _tseitin(), where auxiliary variables are introduced too, while generating some expression, but it's hard to understand (at least for me). It seems to touch the C-part of the library.

Any plans on extending the documentation with some core-design comments which might help people to add new features (even if these features will live outside of pyeda; i don't know, if these cardinality-constraints, which are very popular in discrete-optimization are useful in circuit-design)?

issue_title: Implement support and configuration for SSL
issue_body:
As stated [here](http://openweathermap.org/news/post/ssl-free-and-startup-accounts), it is now possible to use SSL-based communications between OpenWeatherMap API and PyOWM

We need to:
  - PyOWM can be configured to either use SSL or not (default should be: use it)
  - change URI schemas accordingly to what config says (HTTP or HTTPS)

Check out for any potential issue with certification chain verification on the HTTP library/code that PyOWM uses to fetch data from the API

issue_title: Support for creation of user as part of ticket creation?
issue_body:
Does zenpy support this pattern?

https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/tickets#creating-a-ticket-with-a-new-requester

> Creating a ticket with a new requester
> You can specify a requester when creating a ticket. A name, email, and locale id can be set for the requester. A name and email are required. For locale ids, see the Locales API.
> If the requester's identity doesn't exist in Zendesk, a new user profile is created on the user's behalf. The user needs to verify their identity to view his or her requests.
> If a user exists in Zendesk with the specified email address, then Zendesk uses that user and makes no updates to existing users during the ticket creation process. In this approach, only the email attribute is required.

issue_title: Windows Explorer MOVE with non a-z characters
issue_body:
In Windows Explorer, moving a directory from "New Folder" to "test&" fails.

Working:
1. Right click in WebDAV share. New -> Folder
2. Name the folder "abcdefg".
3. Right click on folder "abcdefg" then rename the folder to "test&" (performs WebDAV MOVE)
4. Success

Not Working:
1. Right click in WebDAV share. New -> Folder
2. Name the folder "abc def". (performs WebDAV MOVE)
3. Failure

Not Working:
1. Right click in WebDAV share. New -> Folder
2. Name the folder "abcdef".
3. Rename "abcdef" to "abc def"
4. Rename "abc def" to "hello world" (performs WebDAV MOVE)
5. Failure

We patched this issue in https://github.com/smartfile/wsgidav/commit/17df81a4b8b92c153d7a50a463a60bbe0868545e but we aren't sure if there is a better solution. The patch applies to https://github.com/mar10/wsgidav/blob/master/wsgidav/request_server.py#L833

issue_title: is_mandatory_node doesn't include mandatory "choice" or "anyxml"
issue_body:
Per RFC 6020, section 3.1:

> A mandatory node is one of:
> 
>    o  A leaf, choice, or anyxml node with a "mandatory" statement with
>      the value "true".
> (...)

But:

```python
def is_mandatory_node(stmt):
    if hasattr(stmt, 'i_config') and stmt.i_config == False:
        return False
    if stmt.keyword == 'leaf':         # <------------<<<
        m = stmt.search_one('mandatory')
        if m is not None and m.arg == 'true':
            return True
    elif stmt.keyword in ('list', 'leaf-list'):
...
```

The indicated line should be `if stmt.keyword in ('leaf', 'choice', 'anyxml')`.

issue_title: Better error handling on ISBN conversion
issue_body:
I tried converting some ISBNs like this:
```python
>>> import isbnlib
>>> isbnlib.to_isbn13("91-43-01019-9")
'91-43-01019-9'
```
Huh? That doesn't look like an ISBN13? After some thinking it seems I should have stripped the dashes before trying to convert: 
```python
>>> isbnlib.to_isbn13("9143010199")
'9789143010190'
```
But then again I lose the dashes, that makes ISBNs more readable. 

I would have expected the first version to work, and give me a formatted ISBN13 back (like on [ISBN.org converter](http://www.isbn.org/ISBN_converter)). 

Or am I simply doing it wrong?

issue_title: load OpenLDAP settings automatically
issue_body:
I suspect a lot of people using LDAP already have things like the URI, search base, etc. defined in `~/.ldaprc` or something similar. It would be nice if `ldap3` could use these settings automatically rather than requiring them to be repeated in code.

I’d be happy to add this ability via pull request, but I want to make sure you’re open to the idea first.

FYI, it would follow the same rules as OpenLDAP and check the following places for settings (from lowest to highest precedence):

  * System-wide `ldap.conf` (found in `/etc` or `/usr/local/etc`)
  * The user’s `~/.ldaprc`
  * A file named `ldaprc` in the current directory
  * Environment variables that have names beginning with “LDAP”

Of course, in the case of this library, settings defined in code would be respected above all the other sources.

issue_title: Explicit global _keyring_backend and better dynamic import
issue_body:
Since they matched a closely related functionality in the very same file, those two features were actually merged into a single one.

1) the way _keyring_backend was used in core.py relied on the way the _keyring_backend name was set during import. This could work but could lead to very strange issues sometimes.

The patch provides an explicit global keyword which allows performing the very same task in a cleaner way. NOTE: _keyring_backend was actually meant to be a global even before this patch, but it was used inconsistenly. Looking at all the functions employing _keyring_backend, they will simply crash with a NameError if _keyring_backend is unset, but how is it set is unclear. 

A better approach would be use a registry and not to rely on global, I'll try that in a further patch.

2) Don't reinvent the wheel and use a tested function to dynamically import Keyring backend classes. This patch employs a function from the Twisted project with small modifications.


----------------------------------------

- Originally reported by: Alan Franzoni

issue_title: [Design Proposal] Two separate modes for using tester: Fast compiled mode and interactive/debug mode
issue_body:
Our current test vector interface compiles a new harness every time we generate a new set of testvectors because the test vectors are inline into the harness. Instead, I propose we compile one harness per circuit, and uses a DPI to build a generic harness which consumes the test vectors (in some standard file format) and runs the compiled circuit. Then, we could just compile once per circuit, and emit test vectors via files. The frontend to this flow would be the same, this just changes the backend mechanisms. The verilator target already uses the DPI so that should just work, system verilog has a DPI which we can try to use for ncsim/vcs, see https://www.doulos.com/knowhow/sysverilog/tutorial/dpi/ for a basic example. (@rsetaluri we talked about this before a while ago, let me know if you have any ideas or I'm missing anything important to consider)

A new addition which leverages the "compile once per circuit" strategy introduced above, would be a generic harness wrapping the compiled circuit (via the DPI), which introduces a `peek`, `poke`, `step` interface (basically the same as tester). This would allow us to actually pass through the method calls on Tester to the underlying simulator as the happen (rather than constructing a test vector which is run later). The goal here would be to allow the user to interactively debug the circuit (e.g. get an assertion error or exception on the actual line of the Python file with the `expect` call, rather than later in the compiled harness at some test vector iteration). The user could use the Python interpreter or debugger normally (peek other values that might be useful, etc...).

The main motivation for this interactive mode came from inspecting the Python target, which has a level indirection via testvector construction and consompution, which actually doesn't gain any performance benefit (we're still calling set_value, get_value for peek, poke in python). In fact, we're probably running slower here because we're constructing and passing around this large data structure, instead of just calling the simulator directly. The obvious added benefit here would be the errors occurring in the original python source code for free, rather than use having to do work to map the errors back through the test vectors to the original construction call.

I think having errors map back to the right line when using the test vector interface is still useful/important, but that doesn't solve the issue of interactive debugging where the user can `peek` values that they didn't originally specify when constructing the test vector.

Also, it would be interesting to explore hybrid strategies (run the fast interface up to some point, then switch to the the interactive interface for debugging).

issue_title: Timeout using ssh config ProxyCommand
issue_body:
There is, what I would consider, a bug in the way ssh config files are used to proxy.

Normally your ssh config allows you to do this:

```
Host *
    ServerAliveInterval 5

Host *.domain
    ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p domain_proxy

Host my_host.domain
    HostName 10.10.10.10
```

when you ssh to my_host.domain, it will apply the ServerAliveInterval from Host *, the ProxyCommand from *.domain, and the IP address from my_Host.domain.
like so:

``` bash
> ssh -vvvv my_host.domain
OpenSSH_7.2p2 Ubuntu-4ubuntu1, OpenSSL 1.0.2g-fips  1 Mar 2016
debug1: Reading configuration data ssh_config
debug1: ssh_config line 1: Applying options for *
debug1: ssh_config line 17: Applying options for *.domain
debug1: ssh_config line 45: Applying options for my_host.domain*
debug1: Executing proxy command: exec ssh -W 10.10.10.10:22 domain_proxy
...
```

Netmiko, on the other hand, will not find the ProxyCommand under *.domain, you must set all of the options under the Host its self like so:

```
Host my_host.domain
    HostName 10.10.10.10
    ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p domain_proxy
    ServerAliveInterval 5
```

If you must use a proxy to connect, the first ssh config, without ProxyCommand specified under the host, will give this error:

issue_title: Corrupted Queue in Python's multiprocessing Pool implementation
issue_body:
[Python's multiprocessing pool](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html) has various limitations, one I tried to solve with my wrapper code is the [timeout of worker processes](https://github.com/andresriancho/w3af/blob/master/w3af/core/data/parsers/mp_document_parser.py#L126). I implemented that in a rather ugly way, when the timeout is reached I `os.kill` the process. The multiprocessing pool implementation does spawn a new worker process and 99.99% of the time everything works well.

0.01% of the time there are some strange issues and [the whole pool stops working](https://github.com/andresriancho/w3af/issues/9713), which I believe is because of this issue which is documented in the python docs:

> Warning If a process is killed using Process.terminate() or os.kill() while it is trying to use a Queue, then the data in the queue is likely to become corrupted. This may cause any other process to get an exception when it tries to use the queue later on.

While looking for different multiprocessing pool implementations I found yours, which does implement timeouts and as far as I could read from [process.py#L360-L366](https://github.com/noxdafox/pebble/blob/master/pebble/pool/process.py#L360-L366) there is code to avoid killing a process when the queue is locked. Am I reading that part of the code correctly?

Which other issues from python's multiprocessing pool did you fix in your implementation?

Does your pool implementation have any known issues?

issue_title: Native coroutine compatibility moving forward

As I plan to adopt native coroutines (`async def`/`await`) in my Tornado app soonish, I'd like to know if there are any plans to update/adapt TorMySQL (or even Torpeewee, I use both) to use those? It might even require a fork as that would make both only Python 3.5+ compatible, but it's definitely the faster option moving forward (even more so when Tornado 5 uses the asyncio loop).

There are some gotchas mentioned in the [official Tornado docs](http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/guide/coroutines.html#python-3-5-async-and-await) regarding that switch and I'm not aware how much of it concerns your projects.

Would appreciate hearing your opinion on this.

issue_title: unquote_url_inplace shouldn't unquote in place (or exist?)
issue_body:
The type signature for for the `on_path` function in `bjoern.c` is supposed to take the path as a `const char *` to match the function signature that http-parser expects, but actually takes a `char *`. Right now there's actually a compiler warning when building bjoern about this. The function `on_path` actually mutates the data pointed to by the `path` pointer, since it unquotes in place.

I think the right thing here to do is actually to not URL unquote at all. In my testing of a handful of other WSGI servers, none of them automatically unquoted URLs; I guess the expectation is that whatever WSGI framework you're using will do the unquoting, not the WSGI server itself. So not only is this unquoting thing unhygienic for violating constness, it actually makes bjoern generally incompatible with most other WSGI frameworks. I'm happy to provide a patch to just remove the URL unquoting code if you agree.

issue_title: Add COM threads wrapper classes
issue_body:
COM requires a specific setup for a thread to function correctly. 
\- Interface passing has to be done by marshaling 
\- the thread has to be initialized  for a specific apartment type
\- every create COM object has to be released before the thread has been uninitialized fro COM
\- STA thread join has to do message pumping

This handling is tedious and requires specific knowledge. Hence this patch provides wrapper classes which takes care 
of the needed environment. These classes have the same interface as the standard threading.Thread classes and could be used
in the same way. 

Unit tests are provided using Microsoft Word as COM server. 

Reported by: sschukat

Original Ticket: [pywin32/patches/126](https://sourceforge.net/p/pywin32/patches/126)

Code passes the check

Please provide any additional information below.
I found a similar issue with a patch on the Chromium project:

issue_title: Suggestion: add __weakref__ slot
issue_body:
Hello,

I am using `rewrite` branch, it's working great. I just found that most of `discord.py` types have a `__slots__` attribute. So far so good, however it doesn't have a `__weakref__` entry. This means this code fails:

```python
_event_buses = weakref.WeakKeyDictionary()

def bus(guild):
    global _event_buses
    try:
        obj = _event_buses[key]
    except KeyError:
        _event_buses[key] = obj = MessageBus()
    return obj
```
> TypeError: cannot create weak reference to 'Guild' object

Granted, I could use a regular dictionary and add event handlers to manually delete entries, but it feels inelegant. How about adding the `__weakref__` entry to objects that might be worth caching stuff for? `Guild`, `User`, `Member`, perhaps the `*Channel` class family.

issue_title: Messages sent and received out of order
issue_body:
Hi,
I've noticed that messages sent from KafkaProducer in quick succession are received out of order on KafkaConsumer end.

For example, if I have a producer with
```
from kafka import KafkaProducer
producer = KafkaProducer()
for i in range(5): producer.send('test', str(i).encode())
```
and a consumer with
```
from kafka import KafkaConsumer
consumer = KafkaConsumer('test')
for msg in consumer: print(msg.value.decode())
```
I expect the output to be `0 1 2 3 4` and instead I get them in random order like `2 3 4 1 0`.
I've also tried setting `max_in_flight_requests_per_connection=1` but it didn't change anything.
How can I ensure I receive the messages exactly in the same order I send them??

Thanks

issue_title: Flow control: producer is sending messages when consumers are close to partition end
issue_body:
Hi guys,
I was developing Kafka-based message bus for our [Frontera](https://github.com/scrapinghub/frontera) web crawling framework. The idea was to use Kafka as a data bus to help scale and distribute web crawler components. To avoid generating heavy load and loosing messages because of log rotation in Kafka brokers we decided to introduce a flow control. Worker will produce new requests to crawl only when spiders are about to consume them, but doing it earlier before they face the log end. That implies periodical checking by worker the positions of spiders.

Here is the code doing that
https://github.com/scrapinghub/frontera/blob/master/frontera/contrib/messagebus/kafka/__init__.py

As I understand it requires significant refactoring to make it work in kafka-python 1.0.x versions with new async client. I would like to ask you to guide me how to implement it the most reliable way: using only public APIs, and resistant to kafka-python client future changes.

issue_title: Value error: time data '201801221329' does not match format '%Y%m%H%M%S.%f'
issue_body:
Was getting this message

`Value error: time data '201801221329' does not match format '%Y%m%H%M%S.%f'`

 when running some asn1 through a python program using asn1tools.
Part of the traceback was `"~/site-packages/asn1tools/codecs/__init__.py", line 220, in generalized_time_to_datetime`, so I amended the if else to include a test for string length of 13
and `date = datetime.strptime([string[:-1], '%Y%m%H%M')`

This worked.

This was happening because the timestamp was exactly on the minute, and so did not include seconds.

issue_title: Wording in docs about SecurityMiddleware might be confusing new users
issue_body:
I came across (and answered) [this question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42261467/django-security-middleware-vs-whitenoise/) on stack overflow asking about why they can't use `SecurityMiddleware` and `WhiteNoiseMiddleware` together. They linked to [this section](http://whitenoise.evans.io/en/stable/django.html#enable-whitenoise) of the documentation.

The docs are currently:

> Edit your settings.py file and add WhiteNoise to the MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES list, above all other middleware apart from Django’s SecurityMiddleware:

```
MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES = [
  # 'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
  'whitenoise.middleware.WhiteNoiseMiddleware',
  # ...
]
```

I think the commented out SecurityMiddleware and maybe the term "apart from" might have given them the wrong impression. May I suggest changing the wording and the commenting in the example?

> Edit your settings.py file and add WhiteNoise to the MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES list. The WhiteNoise middleware should be placed directly after the Django security middleware.

```
MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES = [
  'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
  'whitenoise.middleware.WhiteNoiseMiddleware',
  # ...
]
```

issue_title: Provide classifiers for PyPI that specify which Python versions are supported
Move Oauth refresh token out of the config file
issue_body:
Currently the oauth token is stored in the rtv config file as
```
[oauth]
acccess_token=...
refresh_token=...
```
I was thinking that instead of doing this, we could keep a separate key file in the same directory. This would be cleaner in situations where the user doesn't have a config file, or their config file is read-only. Something like

**rtv.oauth** or **oauth.txt**
```
oauth_refresh_token=refresh_token.
```

Could even get fancy and store tokens for multiple users in the same file, e.g.
```
user_1=refresh_token...
user_2=refresh_token...
user_3=refresh_token...
```
idk about that part though..

I looked what PRAW does, they have do have a setting in their main config for the *oauth_refresh_token*. However I think ours is a bit different because we're programatically overwriting the values. http://praw.readthedocs.org/en/latest/pages/configuration_files.html#configuration-files

> the issue in the right GitHub repository.

The module I have been trying out is a Waveshare 1.5in OLED 128x128 unit.  Available from USA Amazon here: https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B07D9NVJPZ/ref=oh_aui_search_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1 
Also, Waveshare documents are available from: Wiki : www.waveshare.com/wiki/1.5inch_RGB_OLED_Module

#### Type of Raspberry Pi
> Not all Pi's are equal at the hardware level - what works on one, might not work on the next.
> This library has been tested on every variant except the RPi3.

Currently testing on Pi 3B+

#### Linux Kernel version
> Paste in the output from running `uname -a` at the command line on your Pi.

uname -a
Linux raspberrypi 4.14.52-v7+ #1123 SMP Wed Jun 27 17:35:49 BST 2018 armv7l GNU/Linux

#### Expected behaviour
> Add a few concise notes about what you are expecting to happen.
> Even better, if you paste in a code sample that demonstrates what you want to achieve.

I've been trying out the SSD1351 luma.oled driver in some python code for testing, but seeing intermittent results.  I believe I've worked through all the wiring and setup issues, and the module is sometimes working properly.  But then other times it will not display anything, or the display will display junk.

I have some local code that works at times, then does nothing sometimes, so I went back to the demos.  For example, the invaders.py seems to work perfectly at times, filling the display and animating properly.  But other times it is half size, corrupted, or black display with nothing.

I am running it with:
python invaders.py -d ssd1351 -i spi --height 128
output:
Version: luma.oled 2.4.1 (luma.core 1.7.2)
Display: ssd1351
Interface: spi
Dimensions: 128 x 128
------------------------------------------------------------

My module is wired as 4-wire SPI.


#### Actual behaviour
> Now add some details about what actually happened - if there is an unexpected crash, paste in the
> [traceback info](https://www.google.com/search?q=python+traceback#q=What+is+a+traceback%3F).

I also tried the Waveshare demo code linked in their documentation above.  I thought maybe the display or wiring was bad, even though it sometimes works correctly.  But when using the Waveshare demo, called main.py in their sample download, the display always seems to work.

This makes me think the luma.oled ssd1351 driver has an initialization problem.  I have not reviewed both sets of code to determine differences, but wanted to post some details in case others struggle with this display as I have been.

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '..')))  # noqa
from watchtower import CloudWatchLogHandler

handler = CloudWatchLogHandler(stream_name='run_logging')
logger = logging.getLogger('run_logging')
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.info('msg')
handler.close()
```

The first time by us, the second time by `logging` module `atexit` registered function, it will hang the program forever, as the thread was already closed, the `exit` message will never get processed.

issue_title: UTF8 source not converted properly under Windows 10
issue_body:
Say we have the following source encoded in UTF-8:

```
pass
print("Café")
```

Then, under the Windows 10 command prompt, the tool running with obfuscation will generate the following UTF-8 file (T is a random variable name in this case):

```
pass
T=print
T("CafÃ©")
```

NB: The `pass` instruction on the first line is there only to alleviate another bug that happens on the first line.

__TEMPORARY SOLUTION__:

I was able to overcome that encoding bug using the windows-1252 encoding format with the source file :

```
# coding: windows-1252
pass
print("Café")
``` 

Then the tool working in obfuscation mode (and all other modes I have tested) will get the correct UTF-8 output:

```
pass
k=print
k("Café")
```

NB. The bug does not go away by changing the code page to 65001 (utf8) for the DOS command prompt. 

__IMPRESSION__:

It seems that the tool takes for granted that the encoding is Windows-1252 under DOS/Windows.

issue_title: Some issues with example music cog
issue_body:
There's an issue here it seems with the `deque` change re: the queue command. I quickly fixed it like this (in the `music.py` example cog):

```py
for i, track in enumerate(player.queue[start:end], start=start): 
```
to:

```py

for i, track in enumerate(list(player.queue)[start:end], start=start): 
```

But I wouldn't say that changing the queue to a list is the most efficient way as it is doing two times the computation and for massive queues it could be slow, but I haven't noticed anything.

It fixes the slice error: `- sequence index must be integer, not 'slice'` on running the queue command.

Also, I don't think shuffle is implemented correctly. It also causes various `- pop() takes no arguments (1 given)` when trying to skip after turning shuffle on/off.

Please excuse any oversights; I'm not the most experienced in Python xD.

issue_title: Customizing autosub to caption TED Talks
issue_body:
Dear @agermanidis,

Thank you very much for this tool. For the captioning process of [TED Talks](https://www.ted.com/participate/translate/guidelines) we have to follow a number of guidelines:

* When a subtitle is longer than 42 characters, break it into two lines.
* Never use more than two lines per subtitle.
* Keep broken lines as close in length as possible.
* Keep 'linguistic wholes' together when breaking lines.
* Keep the subtitle reading speed at a maximum of 21 characters / second.
* Compress subtitles over 21 characters / second. Try to preserve as much meaning as possible.

Is there any way to parametrize your tool to satisfy all (or most) of these guidelines?

Thank you very much,
Roberto

issue_title: twitter.models.Status.media.AsDict() does not contain the key 'sizes'.
issue_body:
I'm porting code from `python-twitter` version 1.1 to 3.1 in [Zulip](https://github.com/zulip/zulip).

In version 1.1, `twitter.Status.media` used to be a `list` of `dicts`. In version 3.1, `twitter.models.Status.media` it is a `list` of `twitter.models.Media`.

We weren't using models in our code. We used to get a dict obtained from `AsDict` to access all data. I thought it will be easy to quickly switch to version 3.1 if we continue to do that.

In version 1.1, `twitter.Status.AsDict()` didn't include `media` by default, so we used to add that explicitly like this - `res['media'] = tweet.media` (`res` was initialized to `tweet.AsDict()`). In version 3.1, media is added by default in `twitter.Status.AsDict()`, so we no longer need that workaround.

I noticed that the format of the dict returned by `AsDict()` has changed slightly. Most notably, the `sizes` key is no longer in the media dicts. We were using that to get the largest image smaller than a particular threshold height. Now I can't find a way to do that. Can you suggest something?

The Twitter API still seems to support sizes, as can be seen here: https://dev.twitter.com/overview/api/entities-in-twitter-objects#media.

Was the `sizes` key intentionally removed? If yes, why?

issue_title: Allow to pass Python callbacks to Browser.ExecuteFunction
issue_body:
Currently Browser.ExecuteFunction (equivalent of Browser.GetMainFrame().ExecuteFunction) is just a helper function that dumps arguments using JSON library and then calls Browser.ExecuteJavascript. ExecuteFunction as API docs state can call not only function, but also object's method (eg. by passing "myobject.method"), or even an anonymous function (eg. by passing "function(){...}"). However currently you can only pass standard data types as arguments. It would be nice to allow passing Python callbacks as well.

How this could be implemented?

1. Store Python callback in a global dict and generate an unique `callback_id` (int)

2. Create a js binded Python function on a web page named `__cefpython__ExecutePythonCallback(int callback_id)`. This can be done in LoadHandler.OnLoadStart. Note that this a bit of dirty solution. There is an another better way to do it, using CEF's V8 extension mechanism. See Issue #391 for details that describes it in more details.

3. ExecuteFunction stores Python callback as desribed in Step 1, receives callback_id and places in javascript this wrapper code as an argument for a Python callback: `function(*args){ __cefpython__ExecutePythonCallback(callback_id, *args); }`.

Tada :)  That should work! Not so hard, was it?

issue_title: Support for bulk email sending
issue_body:
A Relay class which handles mutliple messages per connection. This would be useful when sending bulk email messages e.g in a script or cronjob.

It would be used like this:

    
    #!python
    
    from flaskext.mail import Message, PersistentRelay
    
    def send_bulk_emails(users, relay):
        for user in users:
            message = Message(recipients=[user.email])
            # configure message
            message.send(relay=relay)
    
    relay = PersistentRelay()
    
    with relay.connect():
          send_bulk_emails(users, relay)

issue_title: Colors: Default style suggestions
issue_body:
Added `default_styles` in `colorlib/names.py`, and user stylesheets can be loaded too. Any suggestions (from anyone) for the default styles? Either new styles or different colors/attributes for current styles. Here's the current one that I quickly threw together:

```python
 default_styles = dict(
     warn="fg red",
     title="fg cyan underline bold",
     fatal="fg red bold",
     highlight="bg yellow"
     )
```

Note: to quickly test these, for example `warn`, simply use the following command on the command line:
```bash
$ python -m plumbum.colors warn
```

Also, anything in the library, including 24 bit color, is available, though sticking to a smaller subset of colors (8, 16, or 256) is more universally usable. For example, colorama only supports the first 16.

issue_title: 判断节假日的bug
issue_body:
判断 4月5日是否是节假日 
```
march_first = today + datetime.timedelta(days=1)
is_workday(march_first)  
```
_输出 FALSE_
工作日和节假日判断错误。
原因是 march_first 是  datetime 类，不是 date 类。
但是 datetime 类才有 timedelta方法，进行日期的计算。
将 march_first 转换为 date 类。
示例代码如下
```
march_first = today + datetime.timedelta(days=1)
is_workday(march_first.date())
```
如果可以的话，将上面的的示例代码进行修改。。。。
或者将 datetime 类进行强制转换为 date 类。 @LKI

issue_title: ResultSet slice can steal its parent's rows
issue_body:
Since you give the underlying iterable to the child ResultSet, rows can get consumed by the child without being cached in the parent, for example:
```python
rows = db.query('select * from active_users limit 10')
list(rows[:5])  # all 10 rows
list(rows)  # only first 5 rows, because the rest were consumed by the child
```

The easiest fix is to not let slices go beyond their bounds, which imho makes more sense anyway. I'll make a quick pull request with that approach.

I also have some regression tests for these various iteration issues I've been reporting, I'll open a request to add those too.

issue_title: get_merkle fails for some altcoins
issue_body:
I've noticed that, at least for rubycoin the get_merkle command fails, I traced this to the following snippet:
```
   async def deserialised_block(self, hex_hash):
        '''Return the deserialised block with the given hex hash.'''
        return await self._send_single('getblock', (hex_hash, True))
```
the second parameter, True, tells the daemon to return a list of dicts representing deserialized tx's rather than simply a list of tx hashes. Im not sure if changing this would break things for other coins so I propose the following solution in controller.py:
```
    async def tx_merkle(self, tx_hash, height):
        '''tx_hash is a hex string.'''
        hex_hashes = await self.daemon_request('block_hex_hashes', height, 1)
        block = await self.daemon_request('deserialised_block', hex_hashes[0])
        tx_hashes = [tx['txid'] for tx in block['tx']] if (isinstance(block['tx'][0], dict) and 'txid' in block['tx'][0].keys()) else block['tx']

        try:
            pos = tx_hashes.index(tx_hash)
        except ValueError:
            raise RPCError('tx hash {} not in block {} at height {:,d}'
                           .format(tx_hash, hex_hashes[0], height))

        idx = pos
        hashes = [hex_str_to_hash(txh) for txh in tx_hashes]
        merkle_branch = []
        while len(hashes) > 1:
            if len(hashes) & 1:
                hashes.append(hashes[-1])
            idx = idx - 1 if (idx & 1) else idx + 1
            merkle_branch.append(hash_to_str(hashes[idx]))
            idx //= 2
            hashes = [double_sha256(hashes[n] + hashes[n + 1])
                      for n in range(0, len(hashes), 2)]

        return {"block_height": height, "merkle": merkle_branch, "pos": pos}
```
rather than simply assigning block['tx'] to tx_hashes it first checks whether it is a dict or a list, and handles it accordingly. this should maintain compatability with any other daemons. I would have simply issued a pull request however my fork of the code contains other changes that probably arent desireable for you.

But the `legal_moves` generates moves for them.

```

    print len(chess.variant.RacingKingsBoard(fen).legal_moves)

I assume that the same problem might happen in other variants. A simple check `board.is_game_over()` before generating legal_moves might solve the problem.

issue_title: Not an Issue - more of a question

Based on the documentation is should be possible to edit a record (video or episode). 
I can't figure out how to do this - would like to change the title, sortTitle for a video and episode

issue_title: tag_class has to be a class.
issue_body:
Since we're targeting the _clojure_ data format, why not support tag handlers through simple functions instead of requiring a TaggedElement class?

The nice thing is that if you only want to go in one direction, =edn_format.add_tag(myfunc, 'mytag')= already works.

It would be relatively simple to just take one or two functions instead of a class for handlers.

It would also be nice if the module functions like =edn_format.dumps= and =edn_format.loads= took optional functions as parameters for handlers.

issue_title: AsyncIOScheduler/AsyncIOExecutor shutdown not working properly
issue_body:
I think I have found two issues with `AsyncIOScheduler.shutdown`: 

1) When `wait` is passed as a keyword argument (e.g., `scheduler.shutdown(wait=False)`), an exception is raised: `TypeError: wrapper() got an unexpected keyword argument 'wait'`. This seems to be due to the lack of a `kwargs` in the `run_in_event_loop` decorator.

2) Even when `wait` is set to `False` (and passed a positional argument due to the above issue), currently running jobs (=coroutines wrapped into `asyncio.Task`s running in the asyncio event loop) don't get properly cancelled. In fact, the `wait` argument seems to be totally ignored when using `AsyncIOScheduler`. I don't fully understand the mechanics of the library yet, but it seems it is due to the fact that `AsyncIOExecutor` doesn't implement the `shutdown` method.

IMHO, the expected behavior (when using `scheduler.shutdown(wait=False)`) would be for the scheduler to iterate over all the `asyncio.Task` objects running in the event loop, and cancel the ones which were originally scheduled by a job from AsyncIOScheduler. Something like this:

```python
for task in asyncio.Task.all_tasks(self._eventloop):
    if not task.done() and is_apscheduler_task(task):
        task.cancel()
```
The `is_apscheduler_task` function could be implemented by making the executor keep track of all the tasks it submitted to the event loop and comparing it to the tasks it iterates over. The task creation seems to be [here](https://github.com/agronholm/apscheduler/blob/master/apscheduler/executors/asyncio.py#L42).

Another (quite hackish) approach would be to just simply define `is_apscheduler_task` as: 

```python
def is_apscheduler_task(task):
    return task._coro.__name__ == 'run_coroutine_job'
```

What do you think? In case you agree with what the expected behavior is, I am totally willing to write a PR to fix this.

issue_title: Exception when trying to remove keys that contain an ngram more than once
issue_body:
Attempting this:

    g = ngram.NGram(['aaaa'])
    g.remove('aaaa')

Leads to this exception:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ngram.py", line 244, in remove
        del self._grams[ngram][item]
    KeyError: 'aaaa'

As far as I can tell this is because `splititem` is used to generate the list of ngrams corresponding to the key. These are then removed from `_grams`, with the intention to keep the set in a consistent state (see [here](ngram.py#L243) for details). That all works great until an ngram is encountered the second time. Because the entry was already deleted the first time around it now triggers a `KeyError`.

issue_title: Why does the json database store previous statements as values?
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm a freshman to chatterbot. I have read the code and docs. It's really a great job. 

But I have a question. Why does the json database store a statement as a key, and its previous statement as the value (`in_response_to.text`)? I think an alternative way is to store a statement's responses as  the value. To be more specific, I want to show an example.

A conversation:
```
[
     "How are you doing?"
     "I am doing well, how about you?"
     "I am also good."
]
```

Existing json data storage:
```
{
     "text": "I am doing well, how about you?"
     “in_response_to”: [{"text": "How are you doing?" }]
}
```

An alternative way:
```
{
     "text": "I am doing well, how about you?"
     “responses”: [{"text": "I am also good. " }]
}
```

I ask this question because I find, when the training scale is 2000+ , the response time for a query statement is too long.  I'm curious whether it might be due to the data storage structure. Using existing data storage, each time to get a response, all statements with responses are filtered out (`StorageAdapter.get_response_statements()`) and a statement closest to the query statement is matched. Then, again, we have to traverse all statements' in_response_to value and find the closest statement's response. So, there are three procedures to traverse all statements in the json database. 
In contrast, if we adopt the other storage method, we only traverse all statements to find a statement closest to the query statement, and give its response. I think this way might reduce the response time. 

I wonder whether I understand the workflow correctly. Or, are there any specific reasons we have to use the existing data storage structure?

Thanks.

Handle encoding for numpy array scalars
issue_body:
The `json_tricks` library will serialize instances of `numpy.ndarray` but if you pass it an array scalar (e.g., a single element of an array, which have datatypes like `numpy.int64`, `numpy.float64`, etc.)

For example:

```python
>>> z = np.array([1,2,3])  
>>> json_tricks.dumps(z)
'{"dtype": "int64", "shape": [3], "__ndarray__": [1, 2, 3]}'
>>> json_tricks.dumps(z[0])
TypeError: Object of type <class 'numpy.int64'> could not be encoded by TricksEncoder using encoders [<function json_date_time_encode at 0x7f38028fea60>, <function class_instance_encode at 0x7f38028febf8>, <function json_complex_encode at 0x7f38028fec80>, <function numpy_encode at 0x7f3802410400>]
```

My present workaround is to ensure that all array scalars are converted to numpy arrays before attempting to serialize (`json_tricks.dumps(np.array(z[0]))` in the above) but it would be easier if this was handled automatically.

issue_title: sha256 is relatively expensive
issue_body:
All data is hashed using sha256 to compare the hash on both sides in the final handshake.

Just wanted to note that sha256 is relatively slow / expensive to compute and (somewhat counter-intuitively) even sha512 is much faster (on 64bit platforms and not much slower on 32bit platforms). If one just wants 256bits, one can just throw away half of the sha512 result.

Also there are more modern crypto hashes like blake2 which are even faster, but maybe not that widely available yet as packages for misc. platforms (so that might require to bundle the blake2 code).

This maybe does not matter much as long as one only has little data to transfer or the transmission channel is slow or the CPU is fast.

I don't know how easy it would be to change the hash without running into compatibility problems, so feel free to close this ticket if it is not an option.

issue_title: Stack Overflow (endless recursion) on DeviceConnected._buildPointList with NoResponseFromController
issue_body:
First off, thanks for your great library!

With at least one of my devices, I ran into a stack overflow due to an endless recursion in `/core/devices/Device.py`. It begins in `_buildPointList()` of `DeviceConnected`:

        except NoResponseFromController as error:
            self._log.error('Cannot retrieve object list, disconnecting...')
            self.segmentation_supported = False
            self.new_state(DeviceDisconnected)

The call of `new_state()` triggers line

        self.__class__ = newstate
        self._init_state()

which in turn runs `DeviceDisconnected._init_state()`:

    def _init_state(self):
        self.connect()

After a successful connection:

                if segmentation_supported:
                    self.new_state(RPMDeviceConnected)
                else:
                    self.new_state(RPDeviceConnected)

We again are in `new_state` (see above), which then calls `DeviceConnected._init_state()`:

    def _init_state(self):
        self._buildPointList()

...which closes our endless loop, since another `NoResponseFromController` exception triggers the next round.

As this is my second day working with your code, I'll refrain from posting my "solution" as a PR, as it might not be sufficient for not breaking other parts. However, it goes like this:

        except NoResponseFromController as error:
            self._log.error('Cannot retrieve object list, disconnecting...')
            self.segmentation_supported = False
            self._log.info('Changing device state to {}'.format(DeviceDisconnected))
            self.__class__ = DeviceDisconnected

issue_title: add date plotting support
mac right click is inactive
issue_body:
The right mouse click does not work on a mac with dataexplore. Many mac users only use a trackpad and control-click as a right mouse click, which will not work as the default dataexplore installation. Generally required as macs do not use the same button mapping as in Windows for buttons 2 and 3.

A 3-button mouse (e.g. Logitech) is the easiest to get things to work. Logitech happens to map the middle button to an OS function by default, but this can be overridden (globally) in their settings to be set to "middle button" to get functionality in dataexplore using the middle button. The python application (dataexplore window) may have to be closed in order to make this change, else the Logitech options crashes.

It should be possible to change the key binding elsewhere in the python installation to make this more general, but this is beyond my capability.

Could you add some specific information on the Mac installation instructions to save users time or possibly add a user preference setting for reverse button 2 and 3 binding as "Enable right mouse button (macs)"? 

P.S. The program is great!

issue_title: Message from Nick on the python-ideas thread
issue_body:
Quoting @ncoghlan on [python-ideas](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-ideas/2017-May/045740.html):

>  Some of the key problems I personally see are that attrs reuses a
> general noun (attributes) rather than using other words that are more
> evocative of the "data record" use case, and many of the parameter
> names are about "How attrs work" and "How Python magic methods work"
> rather than "Behaviours I would like this class to have".
> 
> That's fine for someone that's already comfortable writing those
> behaviours by hand and just wants to automate the boilerplate away
> (which is exactly the problem that attrs was written to solve), but
> it's significantly more problematic once we assume people will be
> using a feature like this *before* learning how to write out all the
> corresponding boilerplate themselves (which is the key additional
> complication that a language level version of this will have to
> account for).
> 
> However, consider instead the following API sketch:
> 
>     from autoclass import data_record, data_field
> 
>     @data_record(orderable=False, hashable=False)
>     class SvgTransform(SvgPicture):
>         child = data_field()
>         matrix = data_field(setter=numpy.asarray)
> 
> Here, the core concepts to be learned would be:
> 
> - the "autoclass" module lets you ask the interpreter to automatically
> fill in class details
> - SvgTransform is a data record that cannot be hashed, and cannot be ordered
> - it is a Python class inheriting from SvgPicture
> - it has two defined fields, child & matrix
> - we know "child" is an ordinary read/write instance attribute
> - we know "matrix" is a property, using numpy.asarray as its setter
> 
> In this particular API sketch, data_record is just a class decorator
> factory, and data_field is a declarative helper type for use with that
> factory, so if you wanted to factor out particular combinations, you'd
> just write ordinary helper functions.
> 
> > Instead of trying to cover every possible use-case from a single
> > decorator with a multitude of keyword arguments, I think covering the
> > simple cases is enough. Explicitly overriding methods is not a bad
> > thing! It is much more comprehensible to see an explicit class with
> > methods than a decorator with multiple keyword arguments and callbacks.
> 
> This isn't the case for folks that have to actually *read* dunder
> methods to find out what a class does, thought. Reading an
> imperatively defined class only works that way once you're able to
> mentally pattern match "Oh, that's a conventional __init__, that's a
> conventional __repr__, that's a conventional __hash__, that's a
> conventional __eq__, that's a conventional __lt__ implementation, etc,
> etc".
> 
> Right now, telling Python "I want to do the same stock-standard things
> that everyone always does" means writing a lot of repetitive logic
> (or, more likely, copying the logic from an existing class that you or
> someone else wrote, and editing it to fit).
> 
> The idea behind offering some form of declarative class definitions is
> to build out a vocabulary of conventional class behaviours, and make
> that vocabulary executable such that folks can use it to write
> applications even if they haven't learned how it works under the hood
> yet. As with descriptors before it, that vocabulary may also take
> advantage of the fact that Python offers first class functions to
> allow callbacks and transformation functions to be injected at various
> steps in the process *without* requiring you to also spell out all the
> other steps in the process that you don't want to alter.
> 
> > I like the namedtuple approach: I think it hits the sweet spot between
> > "having to do everything by hand" and "everything is magical".
> 
> It's certainly a lot better than nothing at all, but it brings a lot
> of baggage with it due to the fact that it *is* a tuple. Declarative
> class definitions aim to offer the convenience of namedtuple
> definitions, without the complications that arise from the "it's a
> tuple with some additional metadata and behaviours" aspects.
> 
> Database object-relational-mapping layers like those in SQL Alchemy
> and Django would be the most famous precursors for this, but there are
> also things like Django Form definitions, and APIs like JSL (which
> uses Python classes to declaratively define JSON Schema documents).
> 
> For folks already familiar with ORMs, declarative classes are just a
> matter of making in memory data structures as easy to work with as
> database backed ones. For folks that *aren't* familiar with ORMs yet,
> then declarative classes provide a potentially smoother learning
> curve, since the "declarative class" aspects can be better separated
> from the "object-relational mapping" aspects.

issue_title: run_command with default raw=True, why?
issue_body:
Hey,

It's bad practice to use popen with a string instead of a list of arguments, as argument escaping isn't done in that case.

Why is `raw=True` by default on run_command? This creates all sorts of issues. 

You should simply provide a list of arguments, the executable being the first item in that list, to the popen call. That takes care of proper escaping.

That way, this would work:
`ffmpeg-normalize --verbose --merge --force --acodec libfdk_aac --ebu --extra-options "-threads 4 -b:a 224k -filter:a compand=0|0:1|1:-90/-900|-70/-70|-30/-9|0/-3:6:0:0:0"`

Instead now I have to single quote the compand filter to not get a wrong call to ffmpeg.

If you do not want that (due to popen replacing os.system perhaps?), you can use this to sanitize the argument list to a string:
``` 
import sys
mswindows = (sys.platform == "win32")

if mswindows:
    from subprocess import list2cmdline
    quote_args = list2cmdline
else:
    # POSIX
    from pipes import quote

    def quote_args(seq):
        return ' '.join(quote(arg) for arg in seq)
```

issue_title: Ability to pass attributes to declare_queue
issue_body:
Some extensions require additional arguments for a queue, e.g. message TTL and dead lettering. See for instance https://www.rabbitmq.com/ttl.html. However, `Channel.queue_declare` does not provide any mechanism to pass these attributes to the underlying `spec.QueueDeclare` 

From `ChannelMethodSender` it seems that there's the ability to also pass attributes to `ExchangeDeclare`, `QueueBind`, `QueueUnbind` and `BasicConsume`. Perhaps these dicts should be exposed too.

(There are more specialized arguments that have set defaults in `ChannelMethodSender`. If possible, these may or may not be useful to expose.)

issue_title: If we close connection and try to open channel it deadlocks
Coroutines crush when are cancelled
issue_body:
Basically the Synchroniser must discard cancelled futures. Example:
```python
import asyncio
import asynqp


@asyncio.coroutine
def main_coro(loop):
    # connect to the RabbitMQ broker
    connection = yield from asynqp.connect(
        'localhost', 5672, username='guest', password='guest')

    # Open a communications channel
    channel = yield from connection.open_channel()
    queue = yield from channel.declare_queue('test.queue')

    get_task = asyncio.async(queue.get())
    print("It can go up to here")
    # We have bad connection so get timed out.
    try:
        yield from asyncio.wait_for(get_task, timeout=0.0001)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        pass

    # Next get will hang cause we did not cleanup future in Synchroniser
    yield from queue.get()
    print("but hangs here =(")

    yield from channel.close()
    yield from connection.close()


def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    main_task = asyncio.async(main_coro(loop))
    try:
        loop.run_until_complete(main_task)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        main_task.cancel()
        loop.run_until_complete(main_task)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

issue_title: How's the period (expiry / timeout) information set in the cache
issue_body:
In a previous version (0.4.0), the cache expiry was set explicitly like this in `helpers.py`

    cache.set_many(counts, timeout=timeout)

where timeout was derived by splitting the rate into `count` & `period`. 

In the current version (0.6.0), the file `utils.py` still splits the rate into count (limit) & period, but the period information is not conveyed to the cache.

issue_title: Association only doesn't work due to feature deprecation

My solution was to wrap login_required function to the urlconf:

``` python
    url(r'^login/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', login_required(auth, redirect_field_name=None), name='socialauth_begin'),
    url(r'^complete/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', login_required(complete, redirect_field_name=None), name='socialauth_complete'),
    url(r'^disconnect/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', login_required(disconnect, redirect_field_name=None), name='socialauth_disconnect'),
    url(r'^disconnect/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/(?P<association_id>[^/]+)/$', login_required(disconnect, redirect_field_name=None), name='socialauth_disconnect_individual'),
```

try:

```
fabric parses the `hello=world` and passes it to the config task as a keyword argument. I had to use:

```
@task
def config(action=None, **kwargs):
    """Manage project configuration via .env

        if v:

            key_value = k
    else:

issue_title: Clean way to disable /login endpoints
issue_body:
Currently, I'm managing the users via django.contrib.auth, the only way to log in is via the ModelBackend. But I'm using DSA for social account asociation with those local accounts. 

I'd like to be able to disable the /login/backend endpoints, so nobody could log in to the webapp via social login. What I currently do is override the social_auth urlpatterns in my app's `urls.py`, like this:

```python
urlpatterns += patterns('',
    url(r'^complete/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', complete, name='socialauth_complete'),
    url(r'^associate/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', associate, name='socialauth_associate_begin'),
    url(r'^associate/complete/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', associate_complete,
        name='socialauth_associate_complete'),
    url(r'^disconnect/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', disconnect, name='socialauth_disconnect'),
    url(r'^disconnect/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/(?P<association_id>[^/]+)/$', disconnect,
        name='socialauth_disconnect_individual'),
)
```

Note that I've removed the  `url(r'^login/(?P<backend>[^/]+)/$', auth, name='socialauth_begin')` line.

issue_title: Dynamically adding additional foreign key relationships when using VersionAdmin
issue_body:
We use the VersionAdmin extensively around a large site, and it has some functionality to add translations from parler which are a different, but related, model (we subclassed VersionAdmin and register those models in `__new__`).  I have a few places where I would like to add additional foreign key relations that aren't otherwise picked up.  Typically, one would just register the model with these fields added [in accordance with the docs](https://django-reversion.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#advanced-model-registration).  In a quick test, I can't register this in the admin this way because `VersionAdmin` has already stepped in at this point.  I've seen references to registering in the `models.py` but that doesn't scale as nicely when I have many models (30+).

I'll try to illustrate with a crude example.

```python
class Car(models.Model):
    color = ...
    wheels = ...

class Garage(models.Model):
    cars = models.ManyToManyField(Car, related_name="my_garages"),

class Driver(models.Model):
    cars = models.ManyToManyField(Car, related_name="my_drivers"),
```

```python
class CarAdmin(VersionAdmin):
    ... normal admin things here
```
My thought is to add an attribute to the `ModelAdmin` definitions that I could somehow add fields above and beyond what the `VersionAdmin` does, like so:

```python
class CarAdmin(ExtendedVersionAdmin):
    ... normal admin things here
     additional_reversion_relations = ['my_garages', 'my_drivers']
```
These M2M's are managed with inlines so it would be nice to see changes to them in the change history for `CarAdmin`.

Is there a strategy for revising these registrations?  Perhaps by grabbing the existing relations and re-registering with the addition of those in `additional_reversion_relations`?  Or maybe just directly accessing the `reversion` internals and appending them somehow?

issue_title: Bug with manifest storage and relative URLs
issue_body:
Consider Django's `base.css`: https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.5/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/css/base.css#L356

It uses a relative reference to another resource:
```
background: url(../img/sorting-icons.svg) 0 0 no-repeat;
```

When it comes to post-processing, we end up calling `.exists('../img/sorting-icons.svg')` method in this library. `_get_key_name` returns `shaun-test/admin/css/../img/sorting-icons.svg`, *not* `shaun-test/admin/img/sorting-icons.svg` as expected. This looks to be a regression caused by https://github.com/etianen/django-s3-storage/commit/893608027e8a78dc9b57f783d0c21541cbd2dad2 

I'll try and get a test case/fix together.

**To Reproduce**
Basically any filename with either any of öÖäÄ causes errors. Create a directory with files `oööÖäÄäÄ.txt,` `ØÆ.txt`, `øØæÆ.txt` and view it on the treeview sample. On Python3 the ø's and æ's are ok but the Öä's are not at all well.

issue_title: Searching for hashid's with the ModelAdmin's 'search_fields' feature?
issue_body:
It would be very useful for our daily operations if we could search the hashed value in the standard ModelAdmin search widget.

I naively tried adding the field name to `search_fields` but it doesn't find the items I know are there. This was with an HashidAutoField with primary, salt and alphabet all set.

I've spend some downtime looking into it, and found `ModelAdmin.get_search_results` in the docs, and I can make this search work by implementing it like this:

````python
def get_search_results(self, request, queryset, search_term):
    queryset, use_distinct = super().get_search_results(request, queryset, search_term)
    queryset |= self.model.objects.filter(id=search_term)
    return queryset, use_distinct
````

What is weird about it that my code doesn't do any decoding; the regular queryset lookup on the field does it already. 

So now I wonder if we can make it so we don't need to cart this overridden method around but just use the field name in the `search_fields`?

from matplotlib.font_manager import FontProperties

```

results in an error ("TypeError: 'FontProperties' object is not iterable").

I assume the reason is in the line

https://github.com/ppinard/matplotlib-scalebar/blob/562363b003dd1bee0592ede4c05f6d687fb2f99e/matplotlib_scalebar/scalebar.py#L154
which is 

```
self.font_properties = FontProperties(font_properties)
```

but should be 

```
self.font_properties = font_properties
```

(after changing that, everything works fine)

PS:
Thanks for the package!

issue_title: "No API.AI Client Access Token set. You will be able to query the agent"
allow to modify existing JWT token without changing CSRF tokens
issue_body:
I have a "by the book" (i.e. I applied http://www.redotheweb.com/2015/11/09/api-security.html) 'login' endpoint that verifies credentials and emits JWT in a cookie + token in the response body. The caller is then tasked with storing the access token sent in the response body and using it through a header for subsequent calls.

I have some other endpoints that get used later in the application flow that would like to store more data as user claims in the JWT, essentially using the JWT user claims as a kind of lightweight client-side session system. If I rebuild a brand new JWT, then the XSS/CSRF tokens gets updated, and the client has to update its subsequent calls accordingly.

I'd like the fact there is more data as user claims in the JWT to be kind of transparent to the client, i.e. it shouldn't have to take care that the token values changed. So, I'd like to add data in the user claims without generating new access tokens.

Here is a code sample that outlines what I want to do:

```python
from flask import Flask, jsonify
from flask_jwt_extended import (
    JWTManager,
    create_access_token,
    get_csrf_token,
    get_jwt_claims,
    get_jwt_identity,
    get_raw_jwt,
    jwt_optional,
    jwt_required,
    set_access_cookies,
)
from flask_jwt_extended.tokens import _encode_jwt

app = Flask(__name__)

app.config['JWT_SECRET_KEY'] = 'some random value'
app.config['JWT_COOKIE_SECURE'] = False
app.config['JWT_TOKEN_LOCATION'] = ['cookies']
app.config['JWT_ACCESS_COOKIE_NAME'] = 'at'
app.config['JWT_ACCESS_COOKIE_PATH'] = '/'
app.config['JWT_CSRF_IN_COOKIES'] = False
app.config['JWT_IDENTITY_CLAIM'] = 'sub'

jwt = JWTManager(app)


@app.route('/jwt', methods=['POST'])
@jwt_optional
def jwt():
    uc = get_jwt_claims()
    return jsonify({
        'login': get_jwt_identity(),
        'ext': uc.get('ext')
    })


@app.route('/one', methods=['POST'])
def one():
    access_token = create_access_token('bobby')
    resp = jsonify({
        'step': 'one',
        'access_csrf': get_csrf_token(access_token)
    })
    set_access_cookies(resp, access_token)
    return resp


@app.route('/two', methods=['POST'])
@jwt_required
def two():
    jwt = get_raw_jwt()
    uc = jwt['user_claims']
    uc['ext'] = 'something'
    token_data = {
        app.config['JWT_IDENTITY_CLAIM']: jwt[app.config['JWT_IDENTITY_CLAIM']],
        'fresh': jwt['fresh'],
        'type': jwt['type'],
        app.config['JWT_USER_CLAIMS']: uc,
        'csrf': jwt['csrf']
    }
    at = _encode_jwt(token_data,
                     app.config['JWT_ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRES'],
                     app.config['JWT_SECRET_KEY'],
                     app.config['JWT_ALGORITHM'])
    resp = jsonify({
        'step': 'two'
    })
    set_access_cookies(resp, at)
    return resp
```

1. `/one` gives me a cookie + token
2. using that cookie and that token, I can call whatever protected endpoint I want
3. at some point, I call `/two`
4. the JWT changes, but the token stays the same. I can continue to call protected endpoints with the token I got in step 1

Two questions:
- is it safe to do something like that, or did I just dig a huge hole security-wise?

issue_title: exclude_namespaces is ignored for related endpoints
issue_body:
Issue occurs in views.SwaggerApiView.get_apis_for_resource()

If an excluded url shares a resource path with another api that isn't excluded, it will still get picked up by the above method and displayed.  A 2 line change fixes this, but I can't tell if there's some reason why this would be otherwise desirable.

Example:
/api/things/{group}/
/api/things/{group}/paged/{page}/

If I put the second url in an excluded namespace, it will still get picked up and included in the docs, because the aforementioned method only uses a filter_path to assemble its list of apis, and doesn't check the excluded list.

This is what fixed it for me.
```python
<views.py line 158>
-        apis = urlparser.get_apis(urlconf=urlconf, filter_path=filter_path)
+        exclude_namespaces = rfs.SWAGGER_SETTINGS.get('exclude_namespaces')
+        apis = urlparser.get_apis(urlconf=urlconf, filter_path=filter_path, exclude_namespaces=exclude_namespaces)
```

This change makes everything behave in the way I intended, but will this have unexpected consequences for me later?  Also, is this situation idiosyncratic to my use case or something that should be corrected?

issue_title: Support for reStructuredText in docstrings
issue_body:
Given that reStructuredText (reST) is the *de facto* standard for writing Python documentation (since [docutils](http://docutils.sourceforge.net/) and [Sphinx](http://sphinx-doc.org/) require its usage), it would be nice to have support for reST in docstrings, along with the already available support for Markdown.

Surely Markdown is more readable and its syntax is easier to remember, but reST is more versatile for the documentation of Python modules and is mandatory if you want to use tools as Sphinx.

Having your API documented with Swagger is nice for the end user of the API, but the Markdown-only docstrings prevent you from generating beautiful documentation with docutils and Sphinx.

With reST support in django-rest-swagger, you could have both Swagger documentation and Python/Sphinx documentation, which would be desirable for many software projects.

issue_title: Allow propagation of ignored_fields to automatically generated Foreign Key
issue_body:
Hi, i have some problem with the integration  between django-polymorphic and django-dynamic-fixtures.

This was talked in different issues and some fix has been done like #85.
The problem for me is the automatic generation of a foreign key releated to a polymorphic instance.

The solution suggested in #85 is to set `DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS = ['*_ptr']` but this solution is ineffective in my case, because the DDF code does not use ignored_fields with an auto generated related object.

Example:
```
# settings.py
DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS = ['*_ptr']

# models.py
class Location(PolymorphicModel):
    objects = PolymorphicManager()

class Country(Location):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)

class Publisher(models.Model):
    country = models.ForeignKey(Country, null=True, blank=True)

# test.py
from django_dynamic_fixture import G

G(Country)  # <-- no problem
G(Publisher)  # <-- raise Attribution error
```

The error generated in 2nd call is caused by the implicit creation of a Country for the Publisher instance.
The ignored fields are not correctly set for the publisher country because it receive an empty `ignored_fields`.

I wonder if there is a reason to avoid this propagation, i will prepare a PR to fix it if is not needed.

Carlo

issue_title: Serializer' object has no attribute '_include_additional_options'
Special Characters in query options are not accepted by Shopify's API
issue_body:
If we try and find a specific order by name:
`order = shopify.Order.find(name='#1001')`

Through pyactiveresource.util.to_query (which is used to encode the query string for requests) this is converted to:
`GET https://storeurl.myshopify.com/admin/orders.json?name=%231001`
instead of
`GET https://storeurl.myshopify.com/admin/orders.json?name=#1001`

The former returns an empty list in the response. The latter returns the correct response with an order that it finds.

Seeing as though Shopify uses '#' by default in order names for new stores I would expect this to work without needing to patch pyactiveresource to not urlencode order names. 

Am I doing something wrong? How should I handle this?

issue_title: JWT_Auth Section
issue_body:
I'm currently trying out your JWT_Auth component of the SDK, and I've noticed a couple things. 

1.) Someone should probably correct the fact that there is no "enterprise_token", but rather should be "enterprise_id".
2.) You should probably add "rsa_private_key_passphrase" in there and remove the default value. https://box-content.readme.io/v2.0/docs/box-developer-edition provides a nice script to make a .PEM, yet by default will require a passphrase. It only makes sense to either make the Dev docs not create a passphrase by default, or make a passphrase a component of this SDK.

3.) This is less important, but consider adding references to where to find the enterprise_id. I was searching for 30 minutes before calling support and getting pointed to account settings in the admin console of my "enterprise". Definitely not necessary, but would be a nice thing to have for first time users like myself.

issue_title: bug in parameter default values of gluon.loss.Loss
issue_body:
## Description

(I think!) there is a bug in the default values of the parameters ```weight``` and ```batch_axis``` of the module ```gluon.loss.Loss```. In particular when I try to create a custom loss function derived from Loss I get the following error: 

**implementation that produces error**
```Python

from mxnet.gluon.loss import Loss

class jaccard(Loss):
    """
    Jaccard loss coefficient. Adopted from tensorlayer: 
    https://github.com/zsdonghao/tensorlayer/blob/master/tensorlayer/cost.py
    INPUT:
        tensor of size (Nbatch, Nclasses, W,H)
    OUTPUT:
          The average (over batch) of the average value (over classes). Can I do better? 
          TODO: Use a weight for each class?  See example in tests/Notebooks/custom_loss.ipynb
    """

    def __init__(self, _smooth=1.0e-3, _axis=[2,3], _weight = None, _batch_axis= 0, **kwards):
        Loss.__init__(self, **kwards)

        with self.name_scope():
            self.smooth = _smooth
            self.axis = _axis


    def hybrid_forward(self,F,_label, _output):

        itrs = F.sum(_label*_output, axis=self.axis)
        l = F.sum(_label*_label, axis=self.axis)
        r = F.sum(_output*_output, axis=self.axis)

        IoU = (2.0*itrs+self.smooth)/(l+r+self.smooth)

        meanIoU = F.mean(IoU,axis=1)
        meanTot = F.mean(meanIoU)

        return meanTot


jacc_loss = jaccard()
```

## Error Message:
