issue_title: Translating other (non-standard) models with panels
issue_body:
Perhaps I'm one of the few doing this, but I'm using `wagtail` with `wagtailmenus`, a great package. However, even if the `MainMenuItem` and `FlatMenuitem` models are tagged for translation, the multi-language fields do not show up in Wagtail Admin.

This is because `patch_wagtail_models()` in `patch_wagtailadmin.py` only runs on models deriving from `Page`, or a registered snippet or a `BaseSetting`. Other models that may have panels registered, but they won't be picked up.

So far, the only way I've found to deal with this is to manually run WagtailTranslator on the models in question. This solution works fine, but is quite clunky and not glaringly obvious to most people.

I would suggest finding a way to flag other models, or check every registered models for associated panels?

That said, I haven't yet figured out how to get the `Site` model to appear with translated fields in Wagtailadmin (for it's name). The model doesn't see mot have a panel to monkey patch.

issue_title: Crash on accessing symbols by address in Mach-O binaries
issue_body:
Attachment: [cle_bug.zip](https://github.com/angr/cle/files/1355792/cle_bug.zip)

The current master branch of CLE contains a bug that causes any access to a binary's `symbols_by_addr` property to crash for Mach-O binaries. 

## How to reproduce 

Attached to this bugreport is a zip file contianing `demo.py` and `hw`, a simple "Hello World" executable compiled for MacOS. 
To reproduce the error execute `demo.py` which should produce the following output: 
```
$ python demo.py 
The symbol chosen for this PoC has address 0x1002bc120 and is indeed in _symbols_by_addr
Detected None in _symbols_by_addr, this is gonna crash!
trying to access 0x1002bc120
Traceback (most recent call last):
	  File "demo.py", line 17, in <module>
		binary.symbols_by_addr[known_symbol_addr]
	  File ".../cle_bug/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cle/backends/__init__.py", line 174, in symbols_by_addr
		return {AT.from_rva(x, self).to_mva(): self._symbols_by_addr[x] for x in self._symbols_by_addr}
	  File ".../cle_bug/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cle/backends/__init__.py", line 174, in <dictcomp>
		return {AT.from_rva(x, self).to_mva(): self._symbols_by_addr[x] for x in self._symbols_by_addr}
	  File ".../cle_bug/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cle/address_translator.py", line 73, in to_mva
			return self._rva + self._owner.mapped_base
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'NoneType' and 'int'
$
```
## Analysis

The issue here is that Symbols in Mach-O files generally do not possess an address until they 
went through the binding/resolution process (which may be optional for analysis purposes). 
Also several symbols do not have an address at all AFAIK. 
Currently the Mach-O backend handles these symbols by assigning `None` to their address until they can be resolved, which may happen at various points in a binary's lifecycle. 
The Mach-O backend uses `None` for this purpose in order to distinguish them from symbols that may be validly (but somewhat uselessly) mapped to address 0. 

As a result `_symbols_by_addr` will contain `None` as a key due to the logic in the constructor for `Symbol` (`cle/backends/symbol.py`, line 50-51): 
```
    if (claripy and isinstance(self.relative_addr, claripy.ast.Base)) or self.relative_addr != 0:
       self.owner_obj._symbols_by_addr[self.relative_addr] = self
```

Furthermore the Mach-O backend currently manually inserts symbols after resolution into `_symbols_by_addr` and takes special care to only include those symbols that make sense (compare `cle/backends/macho/macho.py` lines 350-356).
Design-wise I feel that as the backend module is responsible for loading a binary, it is the backend module which should decide where to include which symbol (during the loading process) 
and not a superclass (especially not the symbol-superclass) somwhere up the hierarchy. 

I am uncertain as to how to properly resolve this, a simple fix could extend the check in `symbol.py` to ignore all symbols whose `relative_addr` is `None`. However as stated above I think it would be best if backends had the power to decide what should be included in `symbols_by_addr` (at least during the loading process) so it would be nice if that facility had some sort of opt-out mechanism. 

Alternatively I could also have the Mach-O backend clean out the erroneous keys by wiping `_symbols_by_addr` after symbol resolution but that feels like a very hacky solution. 

As this bug currently halts my work I'm gonna need to fix it soon. 
I'd appreciate any input for a proper solution and can send a pull request once its done.

issue_title: Incompatible with django 1.10 MIDDLEWARE setting
issue_body:
django-webtest seems to be incompatible with the MIDDLEWARE setting in django 1.10, authentication does not work in most of my tests.

The problem is most likely that the middlewares in django_webtest/middleware.py are not in the new middleware format. it's probably enough to make the middlewares inherit from the new mixin, see https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-pre-django-1-10-style-middleware.

my workaround is to use the old MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES setting for now.

issue_title: h11 does not distinguish between bytearray and bytestrings in equality tests
issue_body:
In Python 2 and 3 `bytearray(b'host') == b'host'` is `True`. This results in issues like #60 where users of h11 end up with bytearray's instead of bytestrings. The tests in h11 don't catch these issues because they themselves rely on the python equality operator which returns True when comparing bytearrays and bytestrings that contain the same string.

This can be solved by changing the equality checks to require corresponding fields in both classes to be the same type instead of just checking for equality. I implemented this locally but it broke a lot of tests (since they all assume bytestring == bytearray) so I'm raising the issue here before going forward with fixing them all.

As an example of why bytestrings and bytearrays are not interchangeable: bytestring can be used as a dictionary key but bytearray can not (since it is mutable).

issue_title: Accounts parent_id in records table not being set correctly
issue_body:
Ubuntu 16.04
From source Latest commit 5e2a13b
Postgres database

1. Change kinto.ini to enable accounts
2. Create admin user myadmin with
`kinto.account_create_principals = system.Everyone
`
3. Then change to
`kinto.account_create_principals = account:myadmin`

Final kinto.ini changes
```
multiauth.policies = account
multiauth.policy.account.use = kinto.plugins.accounts.authentication.AccountsAuthenticationPolicy
# Allow anyone to create accounts.
kinto.account_create_principals = account:myadmin
# Set user 'account:admin' as the administrator.
kinto.account_write_principals = account:myadmin
kinto.account_read_principals = account:myadmin

```

4.  Create user using myadmin auth
echo '{"data": {"id": "zzz", "password": "test"}}' | http POST http://192.168.2.12:8888/v1/accounts --auth 'myadmin:test'

5. Try to delete user
`http DELETE http://192.168.2.12:8888/v1/accounts/zzz --verbose --auth 'myadmin:test'`

```
{
    "code": 404,
    "details": {
        "id": "zzz",
        "resource_name": "account"
    },
    "errno": 110,
    "error": "Not Found"
}
```

6. If you change user zzz's password then Delete is a success
echo '{"data": {"password": "asdf"}}' | http PUT http://localhost:8888/v1/accounts/zzz --verbose --auth 'myadmin:test'

(Reposting as my original issue on the matter was closed unexpectedly without resolution)

ZR300 connects and operates as expected on Ubuntu 16.04 on a laptop, however on the same OS, kernel on the Intel Aero Compute board, the camera is not detected (a small number of messages in dmesg, I'll post along with firmware when I'm back in the office on Thursday).

I am using a USB3.0 OTG cable to connect the camera, and have tried over both a powered USB hub as well as directly connected to the USB port. All other USB 3.0 devices tried function as expected.

issue_title: Running workflows docs - strange guidance on vasp_cmd
issue_body:
@bocklund 

At several points in the "Running workflows" tutorials, the docs state:

```
 If your VASP command is anything other than ``vasp_std``, then you'll want to change the line setting the ``VASP_CMD`` key of the configuration dictionary. *Don't use any parallelization software (ibrun, srun, etc.)* it's bad behavior and probably won't work.
```

I don't understand this guidance:
* using srun, mpirun, etc. should work for the vasp_cmd - we do this all the time (although we use env_chk to override the command via my_fworker.yaml - but it's the same thing in the end). So I don't understand the "bad behavior" and "probably won't work". I agree that if you try to run srun on a supercomputing login node you will likely have problems. But that is not what is typically done (see next point).
* the tutorial uses rlaunch whereas the installation sets up an infrastructure for using qlaunch. I am not sure why. Why not just show the user how to use qlaunch? That is what they will be using in production anyway. If your intention is to help the user run a quick test rather than waiting for days in the queue to get the result of the tests, I would suggest to anyway have the docs assume the user is going to run qlaunch (i.e., no restrictions against using srun type commands, use qlaunch -r rapidfire to launch, etc). But in the actual section where it tells you to run the qlaunch command, make a note that saying if you just want to test locally (on the current computer or login node) rather than wait on a queue then use rlaunch instead and make sure the vasp_cmd doesn't include any special commands like srun. But we want the default procedure to be what people are expected to do regularly because that is the pattern they will repeat, and then just make a quick exception for testing purposes.

issue_title: [RPM] Test the --checksum-type repository feature
issue_body:
RPM repositories have a feature that allows the user to request a particular checksum type to be used during repository publish. Unfortunately, this feature has never worked consistently from release to release, which is why Pulp issue 1618 was filed:

https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1618

pulp-smash could perform the following tests:

0. Create a repository with the checksum type set to type A and a feed URL that uses checksum type B (for example, you could configure a Pulp repository to use sha1 but connect it to a feed that uses sha256). Sync and publish the repository. The resulting published primary.xml.gz file should contain a list of all the RPMs in the repository. Assert that all of these RPMs are listed with checksum type A and not checksum type B.

issue_title: Schema.introspect fails when using AsyncioExecutionMiddleware
issue_body:
The instrospect method implies that the return value of `Schema.execute` is of type `ExecutionResult` when it depends of the execution midddleware.
```python
def introspect(self):
    # This fails with the AsyncioExecutionMiddleware
    return self.execute(introspection_query).data
```
One workaround could be to temporarily changing the execution middleware to the `SynchronousExecutionMiddleware` before doing the instrospection query.
```python
def introspect(self):
    old_executor = self.executor
    self.executor = None
    response = self.execute(introspection_query)
    self.executor = old_executor
    return response.data
```

issue_title: Bug: Name of a connection is always the name of the class
issue_body:
```
class BranchesOnClient(relay.Connection):
    pass
```

Based on the implementation of `relay.Connection` I expect the default name to be `BranchesOnClientConnection`.

```
class BranchesOnClient(relay.Connection, name="Foo"):
    pass
```

Now I expect the type name to be `Foo`.

In fact, in both cases the name will be `BranchesOnClient`. This is because `BaseType` sets `_meta.name` to the class name if no `name` argument is passed, and no `name` argument is passed because `relay.Connection` already caught it, assigned it to `_meta`, and removed it from the `**kwargs` dict that is passed on through the ``__init_subclass_with_meta__`` chain.

issue_title: Classification use-case
issue_body:
This project doesn't currently allow for the predicting the type of an input, as there is no sense of knowing to what type an input value maps. 

Normally when using a classifier, there is a two stage process. 
1 - fit(X, y), using training input and output data
2 - predict(X), using unknown data, and returning the estimated 

It would be good if this project presented a similar interface. 

I would suggest creating a class, `wmd_classifier`, which implements these two models.

`fit`, which would:
 * take in an array of documents and break them down into `bow`s
 * create a WMD instance 
 * cache centroids
 
`predict`, which would:
 * take in a document
 * break it into a `bow`
 * calculate its centroid
 * call nearest_neighbours
 * calculate the output type, based on the k nearest neighbours,  weighted by their closeness

issue_title: Multiple microseconds timer support
issue_body:
Current OT only support multiple milliseconds timer, and a single microseconds timer used by [CSMA  backoff](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/mac/mac.cpp#L109) (only enabled by nRF52840 now).

To support the coming new features, we need multiple microseconds timer, the change proposal:

1. Platform alarm APIs update

    - Combine current two alarm APIs ([alarm](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/alarm.h), [usec-alarm](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/usec-alarm.h)) to single microseconds alarm API, platforms which could not support microseconds can still provide milliseconds accuracy but with the same microseconds alarm APIs.

2. `class Timer` update
    Option 1:
    - Replace current `uint32_t  mFireTime` field with the structure [otPlatUsecAlarmTime](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/usec-alarm.h#L61)
    - This option looks much concise, although it will increase 2 bytes RAM space for each `Timer`. 

    Options 2:
    - Create a new `class TimerUsec` based on `class Timer`, with an extra microseconds field.
    - This option will complicate the `class TimerScheduler` to handle the two timers (two queues, time comparison between them), it will increase some code size.

I prefer option 1 more, please let me know your preference and other better options.

issue_title: Review that RTD links point to .io
No Byte order mark (BOM) when creating UTF8 CSV file
issue_body:
A UTF-8 encoded [should include at the start a BOM to specify that its using UTF-8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comma-separated_values#Standardization). There are several csv readers (I think also Microsoft excel) that look for that byte to determine if to read the csv as unicode. Its also [part of unicode(http://unicode.org/faq/utf_bom.html#bom2)

You can get the utf8 BOM like this:

    import codecs
    codecs.BOM_UTF8

You should see ``b'\xef\xbb\xbf'``.
This should be added to the start of a generated csv. If you could point me to where is located I can create a pull request.

issue_title: ISO 639 code for Alemannic should not be "als"
issue_body:
https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/crawl-vectors.html references the Alemannic data set as ISO 639 "als" in the filename, but ISO 639 assigns "als" to Tosk Albanian: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tosk_Albanian

This is probably because Wikipedia incorrectly uses "als" to stand for "Alemannic", even when it's already otherwise assigned in ISO 639. See the section on "language codes" here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alemannic_Wikipedia

Basically, the als.wikipedia.org site contains data in four Alemannic varieties: "gsw", "gct", "swg", "wae". Because there is no single overarching code, Wikipedia continues to use "als", as mentioned in that article. Hence, the Alemannic Wikipedia actually uses "als" as its language code:
https://als.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Houptsyte

However, at the same time, they do tag all articles as "gsw" in the HTML language tags. You may want to relabel your file to "gsw" as they do there. Certainly the word vectors are not in Tosk Albanian, and anyone who downloads only the data files would easily confuse the data sets.

Thanks,
Daan

issue_title: How to handle scoring for meta-estimators
issue_body:
In #196, we came across an issue with the scoring API. For training an estimator on small NumPy arrays, a regular

```python
clf = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
param_grid = {'alpha': [0.1, 10]}
grid_search = GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid, scoring=None)  # or scoring='accuracy'

with joblib.parallel_backend('dask'):
    grid_search.fit(X, y)
```

will induce calls to `grid_search.estimator.score(X_test, y_test)`. This is fine since `X` and `y` are small.

Now, we'd like to do something similar with `Incremental`, (the new wrapper to pass blocks of a dask array to the underlying `estimator.partial_fit`) which is itself a meta-estimator.

```python
clf = SGDClassifier()
inc = Incremental(clf)

grid_search = GridSearchCV(inc, param_grid, scoring=None)  # or scoring='accuracy'

with joblib.parallel_backend('dask'):
    grid_search.fit(X, y)  # X and y are *large* Dask arrays
```

As written, this will end up calling a scikit-learn scoring function with a CV split, which will potentially cause a memory error. The current workaround is to pass a `scoring` to `Incremental` or `GridSearchCV`

```python
from dask_ml.metrics import make_scorer
dask_scorer = get_scorer('accuracy')
inc = Incremental(clf, scoring=dask_scorer)
grid_search = GridSearchCV(inc, param_grid)
```

Then things will be fine, but that may be difficult to discover. A couple issues:

1. (less important) It's a bit unclear how `param_grid` should behave when `estimator` is a meta-estimator. I hacked up `Incremental.get/set_params` to pass things through. Maybe a syntax like `param_grid={'estimator.alpha': [0.1, 10]}` would make sense for scikit-learn? Has this come up before?
2. (more important): The string `'accuracy'` will get scikit-learn's accuracy scorer. This is unfortunate since `X_test` and `y_test` will be large dask Arrays, which will maybe cause a memory error when passed the the scikit-learn version. A few potential solutions
  a.) Do nothing, just write better documentation. Maybe make `scorer` a required argument to `Incremental`?
  b.) Rewrite the scikit-learn scorers to be compatible with both ndarrays and dask arrays (is that possible?)
  c.) A way to introspect an estimator to get the scorer used by `Estimator.score`. Something like

```python
from sklearn.metrics import get_estimator_scorer  # or just put in get_scorer
scorer = get_estimator_scorer(sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier)
# <function sklearn.metrics.classification.accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred, normalize=True, sample_weight=None)>
```

Then in `Incremental`, we can detect that the default `scorer` should be `dask_ml.metrics.classification.accuracy_score`. I haven't checked many estimators to see if this is feasible.

cc @ogrisel, @GaelVaroquaux if they have thoughts on this

Build a proper conf parser that tracks things correctly.  (Preferably one that has a dict-like interface so existing code doesn't break, but that's a lower-priority).

For example, it would be nice to run patch in away that only applies changes and doesn't re-sort and loose/mangle all comments.

issue_title: py::print doesn't work while a PyErr is set
Eventual collisions between opaque STL containers
issue_body:
After some chatting on gitter, @jagerman suggested that I raise an issue here, mostly so that it can be kept track of.

This is related to #439, but has more to do with what happens in the long run when more and more packages are developed using pybind11.

Currently,  opaque STL containers are most often generated with a combo of the `PYBIND11_MAKE_OPAQUE` macro and a call to `py::bind_vector`.

If two different modules by different developers generate the same opaque containers, an import error occurs if both modules are used together.  One simple solution is to wrap the binding in try/catch blocks as shown here: https://github.com/molpopgen/opaque_test.  (That example uses ccpimport to compile the code.)

However, the try/catch solution is imperfect: the name seen on the Python side depends on the import order.  Further, there is the deeper problem that `py::bind_vector` may optionally be invoked with `py::buffer_protocol()` and that the class type returned may be further augmented via `.defs` in the usual way.  The possible result then is that both the name and features/behavior depend on import order, which seems less than ideal.

The gitter discussion ended with the agreement that a solution is needed and that it is not clear what that solution should look like.

issue_title: Problem with syncing and handling a non-color value as color metadata for a calendar
issue_labels: [{'id': 89425274, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/cat:%20server%20compat', 'name': 'cat: server compat', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False}, {'id': 90006607, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/mod:%20DAV', 'name': 'mod: DAV', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 80748616, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pimutils/vdirsyncer/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'eb6420', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This is linked to https://github.com/pimutils/khal/issues/596.

## Description:
When setting up a new iCloud hosted calendar with vdirsyncer, I had the experience of it syncing down a metadata file for the calendar's color with the string contents of "`None`". I have since not been able to reproduce this behavior, but based on the debug log (posted below), this value still seems to exist server-side at least. The syncing of this value caused a crash in `ikhal` (see issue mentioned at beginning of this post) due to not being able to parse the value of "`None`" from the color metadata file. 

Steps (this is as precise as I can recall):
1. This calendar was created in [Fantastical 2 on OS X](http://flexibits.com/fantastical), and was assigned a color when it was created. 
2. I closed Fantastical and then went to modify the configuration file for `vdirsyncer` for it to discover and start syncing this calendar. The initial `vdirsyncer discover` and `vdirsyncer sync` worked successfully.
3. At this point I was able to view the calendar in `ikhal` without issue but it lacked a color.
4. I created the file `color` locally in this calendar's directory to give it color metadata and gave it a **different** custom color.
5. After this point I ran `vdirsyncer metasync` and was still able to view it in `ikhal`.
6. I then deleted the color metadata file and ran `vdirsyncer metasync` again, to ensure that there was a different color being used on the server-side.
7. I got back a file with the contents of "None", which I didn't notice until I ran `ikhal` and it crashed when trying to parse this string.
8. I was able to replace the value in the file with my custom color again and `ikhal` correctly opens the file.

Additional Notes:
* All of these pieces of software use different storage locations for the calendar data for this specific calendar. The only consistent thing here is what is on the server.
    * Calendar.app (Apple software) seems to correctly show the custom color I picked for the cloud.
    * Fantastical 2 doesn't show any color for this specific calendar.
    * ikhal shows the **different** custom color I have selected
* In the attached log below the calendar in question has had the color metadata file remove from the filesystem prior to running in an attempt to reproduce getting `vdirsyncer` to download this bit of metadata. I am not seeing it get re-created, but based on the server response it is definitely there.
* Note that there are two different colors involved in the syncing here, I have different colors for displaying in my terminal vs on my mobile phone's calendar app. The cloud stores the color of the calendar that my phone should have, and i have a local override of the color in my `khal` config.


## Version Info

`vdirsyncer, version 0.14.1`

Server Software:
`Apple iCloud`

Python Version:
`Python 3.6.0`

Operating System:
```
ProductName:	Mac OS X
ProductVersion:	10.12.3
BuildVersion:	16D32
```

Config file:
```

status_path = "~/.config/vdirsyncer/status"

[pair icloud]
a = "local_icloud"
b = "remote_icloud"
collections = ["from a", "from b"]

type = "filesystem"

fileext = ".ics"

type = "caldav"

username.fetch = ["command", "/usr/local/bin/secure-env", "get", "--key:vdirsyncer-icloud-username"]
password.fetch = ["command", "/usr/local/bin/secure-env", "get", "--key:vdirsyncer-icloud-password"]
verify_fingerprint = "F5:CC:B1:A7:24:13:36:07:54:8B:00:D8:EB:40:2E:FC:A3:07:6D:58"
```

issue_title: --dry-run and expiration e-mails
issue_body:
Running Certbot with `--dry-run` can cause expiration e-mails from staging. How?

1. If you do not yet have a staging account and you've provided an e-mail address on the command line or in a configuration file, Certbot will use that e-mail address when creating a new account, resulting in expiration e-mails from staging.
2. If you already have a staging account, Certbot uses it. If this staging account has an e-mail address registered to it, you will receive expiration e-mails (unless you've unsubscribed).

We should fix this, first by never using an e-mail address with `--dry-run` and second by always creating a new, temporary staging account, which is not saved to disk.

`flask-script` is unmaintained, deprecated and obsolete by `flask.cli` which is packaged with `flask` and reuse `click`.

## Possible Implementation
Switch existing commands to `flask.cli` which will allow:
- better parameters validation
- commands testing 
- removal of some hackish imports and circular dependencies

issue_title: Setting parameters to defaults.
issue_body:
I can see no way to set all parameters back to their defaults, without hardcoding the default values in my application. Ideally I'd like to have a method that does this for all parameters at once (which the SCIP console offers via `set default`). I could work around the lack of such a method as long as I have a way to reset individual parameters, or if I had a way to at least query individual default values to then set them manually.

issue_title: Add gas table of common channel operations in our documentation
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

There is no way for (advanced developer) users to easily see a rundown of how much it would cost at any verion of raiden to perform common channel operations.

## Solution

Add a table, probably in [spec.rst](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/docs/spec.rst) with gas costs of all raiden common channel operations which should be updated every time we change the logic of the contracts or if there is any considerable contract revamp.

This way we will be able to efficiently have a versioned tracking of gas costs optimizations and also a way to identify bottlenecks that require optimization.

issue_title: Use of proxy models broke polymorphic / some bad ctypes resulted in no results at all
issue_body:
I came across an issue I wanted to make sure you're aware of -- I'm no longer using proxy models, so this is not affecting me currently.

Let's assume 

```
class Parent(Model): 
   pass

Child(Parent): 
   pass
```

When using proxy models, the polymorphic ctype of a proxy of Child ended up making a new contenttype for ProxyChild in the django_contenttypes and pointing the Parent.polymorphic_ctype to it.   

Now ... I was trying to generate these proxies dynamically, so that could be an issue, i.e.,

```
# From memory ... I think this is how I did it.  Code is gone now.
def _proxy(cls):
    class Meta:
        proxy = True
    p = type("%sProxy" % cls.__name__, cls, dict(Meta=Meta)
    p.save = ...  # I was overriding save on a bunch of these temporarily.
    return p

ChildProxy = _proxy(Child)
```

I see [here](https://github.com/django-polymorphic/django-polymorphic/blob/3af5db0cebbe4267525af76645626b9b8570e0f2/polymorphic/managers.py#L36) you seem to actually check ._meta for proxy, so perhaps this is just an issue with Meta not being properly created above. 

....but this caused a secondary issue which took me a while to figure out.  Since *some*  ctype's were invalid,  Child.objects.all() would return an empty queryset.  Yet I could do Child.objects.get(id=xxx) for some objects.   After wiping the db (it was dev instance), I later realized the root cause is probably that Polymorphic is trying to resolve all of the ctypes.

I'm wondering if this is the desired behavior -- I feel like polymorphic should instead either throw an exception when encountering bad ctypes, or it should ignore those instances.  Instead it appears to ignore even the proper ones.

I can put some effort into trying to reproduce this ... but would like the project's thoughts first.  I think we can probably assume since I see proxy code in the code base that my dynamic proxy generation is the root issue.   And we can ignore that ... 

But do you know of any reasons why a couple of bad ctypes in Parent would make Child.objects.all() return nothing when there are some good entries still?

issue_title: Automatic variable sharing
issue_body: The beaker notebook uses a variable `beaker` to store whatever variable that needs to be shared, which can be easier to use than our explicit `%get`, `%put`, and `--in` and `--out` options of magics `%use` and `%with`. Can we borrow this feature? We can define a variable `sos` (dictionary) to store whatever variables that would be automatically transmitted, or we can simply transmit all variables with name `sos*`.

issue_title: Do not translate variables by default.
issue_body:
I have found it inconvenient to have to put variables such as `ID`, `sample_sheet` etc to `preserved_vars`. In addition, if I would like to write a script specifically for the remote server, I would have to put all variables used in `preserved_vars`.

I think it makes more sense to translate only local targets in `input`, `output`, `depends`, `to_host`, and `from_host`, and leave the rest of the variables untouched by default, because these should have covered most cases when files should be synchronized.

A new option could be named `translate`, or something along the line.

issue_title: Questions about the StackingEstimator
issue_body:
I ran a short regression test with a small data set. Here is the TPOT input:
tpot_optimizer = TPOTRegressor(generations=5, population_size=20, scoring='neg_median_absolute_error',cv=5, random_state=42, verbosity=2)

Here is the best pipeline output:
Best pipeline: ExtraTreesRegressor(XGBRegressor(LassoLarsCV(PolynomialFeatures(RidgeCV(input_matrix), degree=2, include_bias=False, interaction_only=False), normalize=True), learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=2, min_child_weight=4, n_estimators=100, nthread=1, subsample=0.5), bootstrap=True, max_features=0.45, min_samples_leaf=6, min_samples_split=15, n_estimators=100)

Here is the relevant part of the exported python file:
exported_pipeline = make_pipeline(
    StackingEstimator(estimator=RidgeCV()),
    PolynomialFeatures(degree=2, include_bias=False, interaction_only=False),
    StackingEstimator(estimator=LassoLarsCV(normalize=True)),
    StackingEstimator(estimator=XGBRegressor(learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=2, min_child_weight=4, n_estimators=100, nthread=1, subsample=0.5)),
    ExtraTreesRegressor(bootstrap=True, max_features=0.45, min_samples_leaf=6, min_samples_split=15, n_estimators=100)
)

Question 1:  Is the following interpretation of the order of steps used correct?
1.  raw attributes -> RidgeCV -> predictions
2.  raw attributes -> PolynomialFeatures -> LassoLarsCV -> predictions
3.  raw attributes -> PolynomialFeatures (?) - XGBRegressor -> predictions
4. prediction 1, prediction 2, prediction 3 -> ExtraTreesRegressor -> final predictions
- is this using 1,2,3 in parallel then using ExtraTreesRegressor as the metalearning for stacking?
- is PolynomialFeatures applied to both LassoLarsCV and XGBRegressor or only to the former

Question 2: Is it possible to turn off stacking?

issue_title: [xray] Stress test with many dependencies hanging on Travis
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
Travis sometimes hangs on this test when using raylet:
`python -m pytest -v -s test/stress_tests.py::test_dependencies[ray_start_combination1]`. Further investigation shows that sometimes a lot of time (>100ms) is being spent computing a task's dependencies. Most likely this is delaying a timer for the task leases, and that is causing a spurious reconstruction. I'm not sure why the job is hanging, though. Most likely the raylet is crashing somewhere.

### Source code / logs
 Here are some [example logs](https://travis-ci.com/ray-project/ray/jobs/139211770) from Travis. If run on verbose on 170e08c, the logs show messages like:
```
.../src/ray/common/client_connection.cc:160: [worker]ProcessMessage with type 1 took 127 ms
```

issue_title: Actor support multiple return values
ray.init() doesn't have the argument 'ignore_reinit_error'
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: MacOS
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: pip install
- **Ray version**: 0.5.0
- **Python version**: 3.5.2
- **Exact command to reproduce**: ray.init(ignore_reinit_error=True)

### Describe the problem
From the ray latest documentation, it looks like there's an argument `'ignore_reinit_error'` to set when we call `ray.init()`. However, when I do `ray.init(ignore_reinit_error=True)` in ray-0.5.0, it seems unable to find the argument. Wonder if I I missed anything or something incorrect.

issue_title: enhancement - ability to omit values when using JSONEachRow
issue_body:
clickhouse-driver==0.0.10
ClickHouse server version 1.1.54362

Clickhouse JSONEachRow format supports omitting values when inserting records; it would be great if clickhouse-driver would also support this behavior - http://clickhouse-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/formats/jsoneachrow.html

>  It is also possible to omit values in which case the default value of the column is inserted.

However, clickhouse-driver instead throws an exception:

```python
record = {'x': 1}
client.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test')
client.execute('CREATE TABLE test (x Int32, y Int32) ENGINE=Memory')
client.execute('INSERT INTO test FORMAT JSONEachRow', [record])
print(client.execute('SELECT * FROM test'))
```
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "sgm_test.py", line 89, in <module>
    client.execute('INSERT INTO test FORMAT JSONEachRow', [record])
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/client.py", line 169, in execute
    types_check=types_check
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/client.py", line 239, in process_insert_query
    self.send_data(sample_block, data, types_check=types_check)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/client.py", line 261, in send_data
    types_check=types_check)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/block.py", line 51, in __init__
    self.dicts_to_rows(data)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/block.py", line 70, in dicts_to_rows
    self.data[i] = [row[name] for name in column_names]
KeyError: u'y'
```

Working example using **clickhouse-client**:
```
$ clickhouse-client -n --query='DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test; CREATE TABLE test (x Int32, y Int32) ENGINE=Memory; INSERT INTO test FORMAT JSONEachRow' <<< '{"x": 1}'
$ clickhouse-client -E --query='SELECT * FROM test'
Row 1:
──────
x: 1
y: 0
```
I was able to get past the `KeyError` in the Traceback above by changing https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/blob/56395db5a933ee54d9ae8cdebd021f94870899a6/src/block.py#L70 to:
```python
self.data[i] = [row[name] for name in column_names if name in row]
```
I get to the point of sending the data to the server but get the following error, which I unfortunately don't have the time to dig into right now to come up with a possible PR/solution:
```
  File "/anaconda3/envs/tip/lib/python2.7/site-packages/clickhouse_driver/streams/native.py", line 37, in write
    raise ValueError('Different rows length')
ValueError: Different rows length
```
This would be a great feature so that JSON objects don't always have to be fully specified in the client code!

issue_title: Multithreaded client
issue_body:
I've had some issues with using `clickhouse_driver` in a multithreaded asyncio environment. The connection seems not to be thread safe. I'm not sure that is the best approach to solve the issues I've encountered, but here is a pooled connection implementation I am using:
```
import multiprocessing
import asyncio
import logging
from functools import partial
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

from clickhouse_driver import Client
import threading

class ThreadSafeClient:
    def __init__(self, host, compression):
        self._host = host
        self._compression = compression
        self._loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self._pool = {}

    async def get_conn(self):
        tid = threading.get_ident()
        logging.info(f"{tid} - get connection")

        if tid not in self._pool:
            self._pool[tid] = Client(self._host, compression=self._compression, client_name=f"mlcoin-api-{tid}")
            logging.info(f"{tid} - new connection created")

        return self._pool[tid]

    async def execute(self, statement, values=None, types_check=True):
        def execute_in_pool(statement, values, types_check):
            tid = threading.get_ident()

            if tid not in self._pool:
                self._pool[tid] = Client(self._host, compression=self._compression, client_name=f"mlcoin-api-{tid}")
                logging.info(f"{tid} - new connection created")

            conn = self._pool[tid]
            return conn.execute(statement, values, types_check=types_check)

        execute_stmt = partial(execute_in_pool, types_check=True)
        result = await self._loop.run_in_executor(
            None,
            execute_stmt,
            statement,
            values)

        return result
```

issue_title: Versioning of models/views
issue_body:
#1228 is leading to changes in how we deal with model and view version numbers. Here is a proposal:


* Each model has at least 6 attributes:
  * `_model_module`, `_model_module_version`, and `_model_name` describe a model specification, i.e., a list of attributes of the model state, along with their intended purpose. The version number is a semver version string. Typically the version number will only have major version bumps (i.e., any change to a model specification is considered a breaking change. The `_model_module` is *not* necessarily tied to a specific package name. For example, the core Jupyter widgets have a `_model_module` of `JupyterWidgets`.
  * `_view_module`, `_view_module_version`, `_view_name` describe a view that understands the model specification and version. The view module is usually a package name (such as `'jupyter-js-widgets'`). The view implementation used will automatically check to make sure that it understands the model specification. Thus the `_view_module_version` will normally default to `'*'` (i.e., load a version of the view that implements the model specification). If the view loaded does not support the model specification, an error is logged and a basic view is displayed with an error message.

In practice, third party widgets likely will have their `_model_module` attributes actually be a package name - especially if they don't have many different things implementing them. We could also keep `_model_module` to mean a package name, and make a new `jupyter-widgets` package that contains the specification for the models.

issue_title: Reference "reference" issues from the docs
issue_body: There is a lot of really useful information in the closed "reference" issues in github, but there's no real reason for anyone to go look at them (especially new users). issue_title: It's very annoying that fixtures can't yield inside a nursery/cancel scope
issue_body:
Right now, writing code like this is broken:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def background_task_fixture():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        await nursery.start(...)
        yield
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

The problem is that the cancel scope semantics assume that they're strictly nested and can "see" all exceptions that happen inside them, but the convention for pytest fixtures is that we never throw in any exceptions. Also, right now we setup and teardown fixtures in a strict sequential order, but it might be nice to someday do that concurrently, and if we do then this example would *really* explode (you'll get assertion errors inside `trio/_core/_run.py`). (Previous discussion: https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/issues/25#issuecomment-352990630)

For this particular case, that's not a big deal – you can write it as 

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def background_task_fixture(test_nursery):
    await test_nursery.start(...)
```

and that's actually better than the original.

But, as @touilleMan [points out](https://github.com/python-trio/pytest-trio/pull/47#discussion_r204700827), there are other cases where this is really quite a problem. Specifically, cases where the use of a nursery is abstracted away inside some other context manager. For example, consider these two fixtures:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def tcp_client():
    async with await open_tcp_stream(...) as stream:
        yield stream

@pytest.fixture
async def websocket_client():
    async with open_websocket(...) as websocket:
        yield websocket
```

The first one is fine; the second one uses a nursery internally, so it's broken. But to realize that, we have to know what's going on internally in some random third-party library, and that's pretty terrible. So... what can we do to make this better?

issue_title: Add hard size restrictions to initializers and inputs
issue_body:
Currently, inputs and initializers can be overridden, potentially with values of different shapes or sizes. Depending on the graph, this may or may not work at all, and in the meantime being unable to rely on static sizes complicates certain operations on the graph (for example, converting to a backend which requires certain operators to specify their size at graph construction time rather than at runtime).

I propose to add to the model explicit sizes for inputs, which may optionally be inferred (but not modified or overridden) from initializers.

issue_title: some questions about AveragePool pads behavior
issue_body:
hello
I've recently tried to implement the onnx backend for ncnn, and encountered some problems about the pooling pads behavior.
I originally think it is the same as convolution, which takes the padding values and padding area.

example follows
kernel_shape 3x3
pads 0,0,1,1

Input 2x2
```
2 4
6 8
```

In convolution, it will be padded with zeros
```
2 4 0
6 8 0
0 0 0
```
```
2*k[0] + 4*k[1] + 0*k[2] + 6*k[3] + 8*k[4] + 0*k[5] + 0*k[6] + 0*k[7] + 0*k[8]
```

----
I wonder how the padding actually works in pooling
Is padding value always zero?
Is the padding area taken into account in average calculation?
Does onnx always ensure that the Input size after padding is always equals to or larger than the kernel shape? ( input 2x2  with pads 0,0,1,1 and kernel_shape 4x4 never happens)

Input after zero padding 2x2
```
2 4 0
6 8 0
0 0 0
```

AveragePool result (with the padding values and with padding area, likes the convolution)
```
2.22222      (2+4+6+8+0+0+0+0+0) / 9
```

AveragePool result (without the padding values and with padding area)
```
2.22222      (2+4+6+8) / 9
```

AveragePool result (without the padding values and without padding area)
```
5      (2+4+6+8) / 4
```

issue_title: DynamicRNNLayer without dropout multi layer will error
issue_body:
There is some code error when DynamicRNNLayer with multi layer and without dropout .
code
```
        with tf.variable_scope('rnn') as vs:
            tl.layers.DynamicRNNLayer(
                left_embed,
                n_hidden=self.hidden_size,
                cell_fn=tf.contrib.rnn.BasicLSTMCell,
                n_layer=2
            )
```

trackback
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/wuguohua/Envs/py3tf/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tensorlayer/layers/recurrent.py", line 1086, in __init__
    self.cell = MultiRNNCell_fn([cell_creator(is_last=i == n_layer - 1) for i in range(n_layer)], state_is_tuple=True)
  File "/Users/wuguohua/Envs/py3tf/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tensorlayer/layers/recurrent.py", line 1086, in <listcomp>
    self.cell = MultiRNNCell_fn([cell_creator(is_last=i == n_layer - 1) for i in range(n_layer)], state_is_tuple=True)
TypeError: <lambda>() got an unexpected keyword argument 'is_last'
```

solution
```
        rnn_creator = lambda: cell_fn(num_units=n_hidden, **cell_init_args)
```

```
NameError: name 'LexError' is not defined
```

issue_title: nt parser fails when file has only whitespace after multiple of bufsiz bytes

azarot...@gmail.com, 2008-04-17T14:09:20.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Pick any valid N Triples file

Expected output is a graph.
Exception raised: EOF in line

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
Latest.

Please provide any additional information below.
 
Fix:

In rdflib/syntax/parsers/ntriples.py

Line 149, in readline() method:
      while True:
         m = r_line.match(self.buffer)
         if m: # the more likely prospect
            self.buffer = self.buffer[m.end():]
            return m.group(1)
         else:
            buffer = self.file.read(bufsiz)
            if not buffer and not self.buffer.isspace():
                raise ParseError("EOF in line")
            elif not buffer:
                return None
            self.buffer += buffer
